  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
user_prompt:
Write one complete ASHA Worker Note for clinical domain Psychiatry / Behavioral Health.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Kashmiri (code=ks, script=Arabic). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Psychiatry / Behavioral Health — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Jammu & Kashmir, district=Anantnag.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Arabic for Kashmiri.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 59
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Hand Embroiderer, Kamdani
- education_level: Illiterate
- zone: Rural
- state: Jammu & Kashmir
- district: Anantnag
- first_language: Kashmiri
- clinical_persona_summary: A 59-year-old female hand embroiderer from rural Anantnag, Jammu & Kashmir, who is currently married and illiterate, speaks Kashmiri as her first language. She works as a master of traditional kamdani embroidery, managing her craft and household finances while mentoring other women. Her lifestyle includes daily walks, listening to santoor music, and preparing traditional Kashmiri vegetarian dishes.

Document format guide for ASHA Worker Note (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
ASHA note — Village [[VILLAGE|...]], District [[DISTRICT|...]]
Beneficiary [[PATIENT_NAME|...]], [[AGE|26]] / [[GENDER|Female]]
ASHA: [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|...]] | Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|98XXXXXXXX]]
Visit findings: <2-4 short sentences>

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- ASHA_WORKER_NAME — ASHA Worker Name e.g. [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Sunita Yadav]]
- VILLAGE — Village e.g. [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- BPL_RATION_CARD — BPL / Ration Card Number e.g. [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-UP-208001-4421]]
- VOTER_ID — Voter ID e.g. [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]
- RELIGION — Religion e.g. [[RELIGION|Hindu]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
generated_text:
ASHA note — Village [[VILLAGE|Dabhan]], District [[DISTRICT|Anantnag]]
Beneficiary [[PATIENT_NAME|Zahra Begum]], [[AGE|59]] / [[GENDER|Female]]
ASHA: [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Shabnam Bano]] | Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]]
Visit findings: Patient presents with symptoms of depression, anxiety, and sleep disturbances. Reports feeling hopeless and has lost interest in her traditional kamdani embroidery work. Exhibits psychomotor retardation and poor appetite. Family reports increased social withdrawal and tearfulness. Patient mentions difficulty sleeping and frequent nightmares.

Assessment: Major Depressive Episode, moderate severity. No suicidal ideation reported. Patient shows good insight into her condition but lacks access to mental health services.

Plan: Initiated counseling sessions, prescribed antidepressants, referred to District Hospital Anantnag for psychiatric evaluation. Family education provided. Follow-up scheduled in 3 days.

Additional information: Patient lives with her husband [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ghulam Ahmad]]. She holds [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-JK-2024-001234]] and [[VOTER_ID|JK1234567]]. Patient is [[RELIGION|Muslim]].

professional_persona: Kaash Mari, a 59‑year‑old master of kamdani hand embroidery, combines meticulous stitch precision with an exceptionally organized workflow, delivering intricate silk‑and‑zari motifs while they assertively mentor apprentices and oversee a budding artisan cooperative.
linguistic_persona: Kaash Mari speaks her mother tongue, a regional valley language, fluently at home, grasps Hindi from radio and television, and picks up basic Urdu for market chats, leveraging this practical multilingual ability to negotiate sales, teach stitching, and satisfy their curious mind, even though they cannot read or write.
sports_persona: Kaash Mari enjoys daily walks with the local women’s group ‘Roshni Walkers’, follows the Delhi Capitals in the IPL on TV, cheers for the Indian women’s national football team, and they practice gentle yoga at the temple courtyard, blending their disciplined routine with light exercise.
arts_persona: Kaash Mari regularly listens to santoor maestro Shivkumar Sharma and a renowned regional folk singer, revels in the verses of a beloved poet of the valley, and they participate in community storytelling evenings where legends like "The Tale of the Lotus" are narrated, letting their artistic soul intertwine with their embroidery designs.
travel_persona: Kaash Mari prefers modest weekend getaways to hill stations such as Shimla and Darjeeling, dreams of a cultural tour of Jaipur’s heritage bazaars, and they plan trips with their children’s assistance, balancing their love for new experiences with a practical, budget‑conscious approach.
culinary_persona: Kaash Mari delights in preparing aromatic vegetarian Rogan Josh with fragrant rosemary, simmered dum Aloo, and brewing spiced traditional tea, while they often decorate platters with fresh marigold petals, showcasing their love for traditional flavors and garden‑fresh ingredients despite occasional forgetfulness in timing the spices.
persona: Kaash Mari, a disciplined 59‑year‑old embroidery master, blends curiosity with practicality, expertly preserving age‑old kamdani art while they mentor women, manage finances prudently, and savor garden‑grown aromas and folk melodies.
cultural_background: Kaash Mari belongs to the Kashmiri Hindu community of Anantnag, a rural region in the Kashmir Valley. Her cultural identity is shaped by the valley’s rich traditions of poetry, music, and especially the art of kamdani embroidery, which has been passed down through generations of Hindu families. Despite being a minority in a predominantly Muslim region, Kashmiri Hindus maintain distinct festivals such as Navratri, Shivratri, and celebrate daily rituals at the local temple. Life in rural Anantnag revolves around seasonal agricultural cycles, close-knit village ties, and a strong emphasis on preserving handcrafted heritage.
linguistic_background: Kaash’s mother tongue is Kashmiri, which she speaks fluently and uses daily for household communication, storytelling, and passing on embroidery techniques. Though she is illiterate, she has a functional listening comprehension of Hindi through regional radio and television, and can understand basic Urdu, the lingua franca of the valley, especially in market settings. She does not read or write in any script, but her oral language skills enable her to negotiate with customers, teach apprentices, and participate actively in community gatherings.
skills_and_expertise: Kaash is a master of Kashmiri kamdani hand embroidery, capable of creating intricate motifs using silk, zari, and cotton threads. She excels in traditional design creation, colour coordination, stitch precision, and finishing techniques that meet high aesthetic standards. Her organizational skills enable her to manage inventory of yarns, tools, and finished pieces, track orders, and maintain a disciplined workflow. Over the years she has mentored younger women in the village, teaching them embroidery fundamentals and business practices, and she handles customer relations with courteous professionalism.
skills_and_expertise_list: ['Kashmiri kamdani hand embroidery', 'Intricate silk and zari stitch work', 'Traditional motif and pattern design', 'Colour coordination and palette selection', 'Precision stitching and finishing', 'Inventory management of yarns and tools', 'Order tracking and deadline adherence', 'Mentoring apprentices in embroidery', 'Customer relationship management']
hobbies_and_interests: Kaash enjoys listening to Kashmiri folk songs and the soothing strains of the santoor during evenings, often while sipping kahwa. She loves gardening, especially growing rosemary and marigold around her courtyard. Traditional cooking, particularly preparing Rogan Josh and its vegetarian variations, is a cherished pastime. She participates in local temple festivals, shares stories with fellow women, and spends time teaching younger relatives basic embroidery stitches. Occasionally, she attends community storytelling sessions where Kashmiri legends are narrated.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ['Listening to Kashmiri folk music', 'Enjoying santoor melodies', 'Gardening (rosemary, marigold)', 'Traditional Kashmiri cooking (Rogan Josh, vegetarian dishes)', 'Participating in temple festivals', 'Storytelling with fellow women', 'Teaching embroidery basics to younger relatives', 'Attending community storytelling sessions']
career_goals_and_ambitions: Kaash aspires to preserve the kamdani tradition by establishing a small cooperative that trains the next generation of women artisans in Anantnag. She aims to expand her market reach beyond the local bazaars, seeking participation in state-level handicraft exhibitions and exploring online platforms through the assistance of her children. Her ambition includes receiving recognition such as a State Handicraft Award, and eventually authoring a visual guide on kamdani techniques to document her lifelong expertise.
persona_clinical_summary: A 59-year-old female hand embroiderer from rural Anantnag, Jammu & Kashmir, who is currently married and illiterate, speaks Kashmiri as her first language. She works as a master of traditional kamdani embroidery, managing finances and mentoring others while maintaining a disciplined lifestyle. Her daily routine includes walks with a local women's group and listening to regional folk music.
system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

Patient presented with complaints of persistent fever, body ache, and fatigue for 5 days. General examination revealed mild dehydration and elevated temperature of 102.4°F. Blood tests showed elevated WBC count. Patient was diagnosed with viral fever and prescribed paracetamol, ORS, and rest. Admitted for observation and discharged after 48 hours with follow-up instructions.

Claim Amount: Rs. 25,000 for hospitalization and medication.

professional_persona: Dayanand Baitha, an 18‑year‑old agricultural assistant in Saharsa, blends hands‑on field skills like sowing, weeding and basic carpentry with a calm, practical curiosity, making him a reliable yet adaptable contributor to his family's farm and future dairy venture.
linguistic_persona: Dayanand Baitha, a native Maithili speaker who understands spoken Hindi at a basic level but reads and writes only minimally, relies on oral conversation and proverb sharing to communicate effectively.
sports_persona: Dayanand Baitha enjoys playing gilli‑danda in the village fields, follows the Indian cricket team and the Bihar Ranji side on TV, and occasionally joins a local cricket club where he bowls medium‑pace deliveries during weekend practice, reflecting his balance of casual play and modest fitness.
arts_persona: Dayanand Baitha often sings Maithili folk songs popularized by Sharda Sinha, plays the dholak at gatherings, and admires the intricate Madhubani paintings of legendary artist Mahasundari Devi, integrating these arts into his daily rhythm.
travel_persona: Dayanand Baitha prefers modest trips, often traveling by bus to nearby pilgrimage sites such as Vaishali and Varanasi, dreams of seeing the Himalayan foothills of Darjeeling and the cultural mosaic of Kolkata, planning trips with family support and limited budget, balancing curiosity and practicality.
culinary_persona: Dayanand Baitha enjoys hearty Maithili meals like litti‑chokha, sattu‑paratha, and pua, using mustard oil, panch‑phoran spices and fresh river fish, and while he can rustle up these dishes with confidence, he often prefers the comforting taste of home‑cooked dal‑bhat over trying new cuisines.
persona: Dayanand Baitha, an 18‑year‑old farmhand who balances a calm, resilient spirit with a curious love for folk songs and a habit of humming them even while sowing, yet often forgets to attend adult‑education classes despite his eagerness to learn.
cultural_background: Dayanand belongs to the Maithili community of the Mithila region in Saharsa, Bihar. His upbringing is steeped in the rural customs of the Gangetic plains, where agricultural cycles, folk traditions such as Madhubani painting, and seasonal festivals shape daily life. He values the collective spirit of joint families and the pragmatic wisdom passed down through generations.
linguistic_background: Dayanand's mother tongue is Maithili, in which he converses fluently. He has developed a basic ability to understand spoken Hindi, but his literacy is limited; having studied below the primary level, his writing skills are rudimentary.
skills_and_expertise: He is skilled in basic agricultural tasks such as sowing, weeding, and assisting with harvest. He knows how to manage the family kitchen, cooking traditional Maithili dishes, and handling household chores. He can also perform simple carpentry, rope making, and has a good memory for folk songs and proverbs, which helps him preserve oral tradition.

He dreams of becoming a self‑reliant contributor to his village’s development without compromising his cultural roots.

system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):
  [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|157815659387]]
  [[ABHA_ADDRESS|meena.devi@abdm]]
  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

  [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-77821]]

  [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]

  [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]

  [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]
  [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]
  [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
user_prompt:
Write one complete Prescription for clinical domain Oncology / Complex Chronic Care.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Maithili (code=mai, script=Devanagari). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Oncology / Complex Chronic Care — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Bihar, district=Saharsa.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Devanagari for Maithili.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 18
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Below Primary
- zone: Rural
- state: Bihar
- district: Saharsa
- first_language: Maithili
- clinical_persona_summary: An 18‑year‑old male from Saharsa, Bihar, currently married, with below‑primary education and a rural background. His first language is Maithili, and he works as a farmhand while exhibiting limited health‑literacy. He has a calm demeanor but may miss adult‑education appointments despite expressing interest in learning.

Document format guide for Prescription (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Prescription — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]]
Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|...]], [[AGE|28]] / [[GENDER|Male]], MRN [[MRN|RX-1001]]
Dr. [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Rx: <drug dose frequency>  (do NOT tag drug names as entities)

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS — Residential Address e.g. [[RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS|12 Gandhi Nagar, Ward 4]]
- PATIENT_ID — Patient ID e.g. [[PATIENT_ID|PID-77821]]
- ABHA_ID — ABHA ID e.g. [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
generated_text:
Prescription — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Saharsa District Hospital]]
Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|Ramesh Kumar]], [[AGE|18]] / [[GENDER|Male]], MRN [[MRN|MRN-ONC-2024-001]]
Dr. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. S. K. Jha]]
Diagnosis: Stage IIA Hodgkin's Lymphoma. Patient counseled regarding chemotherapy regimen.
Rx:
1. Cyclophosphamide 500mg/m² IV on Day 1
2. Doxorubicin 25mg/m² IV on Day 1
3. Vincristine 1.4mg/m² IV on Day 1 (max 2mg)
4. Prednisone 100mg PO daily for 5 days, starting Day 1
5. Ondansetron 8mg PO/IV q8h PRN for nausea
Administer chemotherapy on an outpatient basis. Monitor CBC and renal function before each cycle. Patient to follow up in Oncology OPD in 7 days.
For queries, contact Oncology Ward at [[PHONE_NUMBER|9431023456]].
[[PATIENT_ID|PID-ONC-789]] | [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]] | [[RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS|Village: Mahua, Post: Saur Bazar, Saharsa, Bihar - 852201]] | [[DISTRICT|Saharsa]]

GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.

user_prompt:
Write one complete Lab Report for clinical domain Psychiatry / Behavioral Health.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Maithili (code=mai, script=Devanagari). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Psychiatry / Behavioral Health — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Bihar, district=Saharsa.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Devanagari for Maithili.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 18
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Below Primary
- zone: Rural
- state: Bihar
- district: Saharsa
- first_language: Maithili
- clinical_persona_summary: An 18‑year‑old male from Saharsa, Bihar, currently married, with below primary education and a rural background. He speaks Maithili as his first language and has limited literacy in Hindi. His occupation is currently a farmhand, and he is a homemaker.

Document format guide for Lab Report (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton (English pivot; TYPE names stay ASCII forever):
Lab report — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Barddhaman District Hospital]] District [[DISTRICT|Purba Bardhaman]]
MRN [[MRN|MRN-WB-2024-0815]] Patient ID [[PATIENT_ID|PID-WB-7781]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] [[AGE|27]] [[GENDER|Female]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]]

Results table (analyte values NOT tagged as PHI entities).
NEVER write [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] / [[এম.আর.এন|…]] — only PATIENT_NAME, MRN, etc.

Lab Report — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Saharsa District Hospital]] District [[DISTRICT|Saharsa]]
MRN [[MRN|MRN-BIH-2024-0815-001]] Patient ID [[PATIENT_ID|PID-BIH-7781]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Ramesh Kumar]] [[AGE|18]] [[GENDER|Male]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]]
Ordered by [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Suman Kumar]]
Chief Complaint: Patient presents with persistent low mood, anxiety, and difficulty adjusting to recent life changes.
History of Present Illness: The patient is an 18-year-old male from a rural background who reports feeling sad and hopeless for the past three months. He describes a loss of interest in his daily activities, including work on the family farm. He endorses symptoms of anxiety, including restlessness and excessive worry about his new responsibilities after marriage. Sleep is disturbed with early morning awakening, and appetite has decreased. The patient has limited literacy and reports these feelings using simple terms.
Assessment: Adjustment Disorder with Mixed Anxiety and Depressed Mood.
Plan: 1. Initiate supportive psychotherapy sessions focusing on coping strategies for stress. 2. Consider a trial of Sertraline 50mg daily, with close monitoring for side effects. 3. Baseline laboratory investigations ordered to rule out organic contributors. 4. Follow-up in one week to assess response and side effects.
Investigations:
CBC: Hemoglobin 13.5 g/dL, WBC 7,500/mm3, Platelets 250,000/mm3.
LFT: Within normal limits.
RFT: Creatinine 0.9 mg/dL, Urea 20 mg/dL.
TFT: TSH 2.1 mIU/L, T3 and T4 within normal limits.
[[RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS|Shivpur, Saharsa, Bihar]]
[[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]

professional_persona: Dayanand Baitha, an 18‑year‑old agricultural assistant in Saharsa, blends hands‑on field skills like sowing, weeding and basic carpentry with a calm, practical curiosity, making him a reliable yet adaptable contributor to his family's farm and future dairy venture.
linguistic_persona: Dayanand Baitha, a native Maithili speaker who understands spoken Hindi at a basic level but reads and writes only minimally, relies on oral conversation and proverb sharing to communicate effectively.
sports_persona: Dayanand Baitha enjoys playing gilli‑danda in the village fields, follows the Indian cricket team and the Bihar Ranji side on TV, and occasionally joins a local cricket club where he bowls medium‑pace deliveries during weekend practice, reflecting his balance of casual play and modest fitness.
arts_persona: Dayanand Baitha often sings Maithili folk songs popularized by Sharda Sinha, plays the dholak at gatherings, and admires the intricate Madhubani paintings of legendary artist Mahasundari Devi, integrating these arts into his daily rhythm.
travel_persona: Dayanand Baitha prefers modest trips, often traveling by bus to nearby pilgrimage sites such as Vaishali and Varanasi, dreams of seeing the Himalayan foothills of Darjeeling and the cultural mosaic of Kolkata, planning trips with family support and limited budget, balancing curiosity and practicality.
culinary_persona: Dayanand Baitha enjoys hearty Maithili meals like litti‑chokha, sattu‑paratha, and pua, using mustard oil, panch‑phoran spices and fresh river fish, and while he can rustle up these dishes with confidence, he often prefers the comforting taste of home‑cooked dal‑bhat over trying new cuisines.
persona: Dayanand Baitha, an 18‑year‑old farmhand who balances a calm, resilient spirit with a curious love for folk songs and a habit of humming them even while sowing, yet often forgets to attend adult‑education classes despite his eagerness to learn.
cultural_background: Dayanand belongs to the Maithili community of the Mithila region in Saharsa, Bihar. His upbringing is steeped in the rural customs of the Gangetic plains, where agricultural cycles, folk traditions such as Madhubani painting, and seasonal festivals shape daily life. He values the collective spirit of joint families and the pragmatic wisdom passed down through generations.
linguistic_background: Dayanand's mother tongue is Maithili, in which he converses fluently. He has developed a basic ability to understand spoken Hindi, but his literacy is limited; having studied below the primary level, his writing skills are rudimentary.
skills_and_expertise: He is skilled in basic agricultural tasks such as sowing, weeding, and assisting with harvest. He knows how to manage the family kitchen, cooking traditional Maithili dishes, and handling household chores. He can also perform simple carpentry, rope making, and has a good memory for folk songs and proverbs, which helps him preserve oral tradition.
skills_and_expertise_list: ['Sowing and weeding', 'Crop harvesting assistance', 'Traditional Maithili cooking', 'Household management', 'Basic carpentry', 'Rope making', 'Oral storytelling', 'Folk song memorization']
hobbies_and_interests: In his spare time, Dayanand enjoys listening to Maithili folk songs and bhajans, often humming while working in the fields. He likes to play the dholak at village gatherings and participates in traditional games such as gilli‑danda. He also spends evenings reciting proverbs and riddles with his wife, preserving oral traditions. Occasionally, he watches cricket matches on a communal television when available.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ['Listening to Maithili folk songs', 'Playing dholak', 'Gilli-danda', 'Reciting proverbs and riddles', 'Watching cricket on community TV']
career_goals_and_ambitions: Dayanand aspires to acquire basic literacy through adult education programs and to learn practical skills such as animal husbandry and small‑scale agribusiness. Over the next few years, he hopes to set up a modest dairy unit or vegetable stall to supplement his family’s income, while also mentoring younger siblings in Maithili traditions. He dreams of becoming a self‑reliant contributor to his village’s development without compromising his cultural roots.
persona_clinical_summary: An 18-year-old male from Saharsa, Bihar, currently married, with below-primary education and a rural background. He speaks Maithili as his first language and has limited Hindi comprehension, often missing adult-education classes. His lifestyle includes agricultural work, a habit of humming folk songs, and occasional participation in local gilli-danda games.
system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

Document format guide for ASHA Worker Note (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):

professional_persona: Tetri Devi, an organized village event coordinator with a flair for Madhubani painting, channels their disciplined mindset and sociable energy into overseeing festivals and launching a home‑based art and pickles venture.
linguistic_persona: Tetri Devi, a native Maithili speaker with basic reading and writing skills, converses fluently in Hindi and picks up borrowed English words from television, though they cannot yet read or write English confidently.
sports_persona: Tetri Devi enjoys watching cricket on television, they cheer for the Bihar cricket team during domestic matches, and they occasionally participate in village kabaddi sessions at the community ground to stay fit.
arts_persona: Tetri Devi creates vibrant Madhubani paintings inspired by folk tales, they sing along to Maithili and Bhojpuri tracks by Sharda Sinha, and they collect handmade crafts from neighboring villages, weaving these artistic influences into their daily life.
travel_persona: Tetri Devi prefers short road trips to nearby towns like Patna and Darbhanga for market days, they dream of visiting Varanasi’s ghats and Delhi’s museums, and they plan to explore neighboring states during festival seasons when travel is affordable.
culinary_persona: Tetri Devi prepares traditional Maithili dishes such as litti‑chokha, fish curry with mustard seed paste, and dhuska, enjoys the aroma of cumin and fenugreek in their kitchen, and prefers dining at family gatherings where bharta and makhana are served.
persona: Tetri Devi, an imaginative 22‑year‑old village organizer who blends disciplined budgeting, vibrant Madhubani art, and community cheerleading into a lively, slightly impulsive yet purpose‑driven life, and their infectious enthusiasm keeps neighbours engaged.
cultural_background: Tetri hails from the Maithil community in Araria, a rural part of the Mithila region in Bihar. Her upbringing is rooted in the vibrant cultural fabric of Mithila, renowned for folk songs, Madhubani art, and seasonal festivals aligned with the agricultural calendar. Growing up in a joint family, she respects traditional customs while also embracing curiosity about new ideas and artistic expressions. Her sociable nature makes her an active participant in village gatherings, melas, weddings, and other community events, often taking a leading role in organizing and adding a touch of creativity to celebrations.
linguistic_background: Linguistic background: Tetri's mother tongue is Maithili, which she speaks fluently and can read and write at a basic level despite having completed less than primary schooling. She is also conversationally proficient in Hindi, the lingua franca of Bihar, and can understand spoken Hindi well, though her Devanagari reading and writing skills are rudimentary. Her exposure to English is minimal, limited to a handful of borrowed words from television and radio; she cannot read or write English with confidence.
skills_and_expertise: Skills and expertise: Tetri excels at managing household chores, preparing traditional Maithili dishes, and budgeting modest family expenditures. Her disciplined mindset makes her an effective organizer for festivals and family functions, ensuring everything runs smoothly. She possesses artistic talent in Madhubani painting, as well as practical skills in sewing, embroidery, and crafting simple handmade items. Her confident communication style also enables her to serve as a liaison in community affairs and coordinate collective activities.

hobbies_and_interests: Hobbies and interests: Tetri enjoys listening to folk music, especially Maithili and Bhojpuri songs, and often sings along. career_goals_and_ambitions: Tetri aims to deepen her family's prosperity by mastering household finance management, pursuing adult literacy, and channeling her artistic flair into a small home‑based venture selling Madhubani paintings and traditional pickles. system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):
  [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|157815659387]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
user_prompt:
Write one complete Lab Report for clinical domain Oncology / Complex Chronic Care.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Maithili (code=mai, script=Devanagari). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Oncology / Complex Chronic Care — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Bihar, district=Araria.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Devanagari for Maithili.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 22
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Below Primary
- zone: Rural
- state: Bihar
- district: Araria
- first_language: Maithili

She speaks Maithili as her first language and is proficient in Hindi. Her lifestyle includes community organizing and Madhubani art, with a diet centered on traditional Maithili cuisine.

Document format guide for Lab Report (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):

Lab report — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Barddhaman District Hospital]] District [[DISTRICT|Purba Bardhaman]]
MRN [[MRN|MRN-WB-2024-0815]] Patient ID [[PATIENT_ID|PID-WB-7781]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] [[AGE|27]] [[GENDER|Female]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]]
Ordered by [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Riya Banerjee]]
Results table (analyte values NOT tagged as PHI entities).
NEVER write [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] / [[এম.আর.এন|…]] — only PATIENT_NAME, MRN, etc.

MANDATORY inline entities (9 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- PATIENT_ID — Patient ID e.g. [[PATIENT_ID|PID-77821]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

[[PATIENT_NAME|Sita Devi]] [[AGE|22]] [[GENDER|Female]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]]
Ordered by [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rajesh Kumar]]

Patient presents with persistent abdominal pain, weight loss, and fatigue for the past three months. Family history significant for maternal aunt with ovarian cancer. Physical examination reveals abdominal tenderness and palpable mass in the right lower quadrant.

LABORATORY FINDINGS:
Complete Blood Count:
- Hemoglobin: 9.8 g/dL (Low)
- White Blood Cell Count: 7.2 x 10^9/L (Normal)
- Platelet Count: 245 x 10^9/L (Normal)

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
- Total Protein: 6.8 g/dL (Low)
- Albumin: 3.2 g/dL (Low)
- Calcium: 8.9 mg/dL (Normal)
- Liver Function Tests: Elevated AST and ALT
- Renal Function: Within normal limits

Tumor Markers:
- CA-125: 185 U/mL (Elevated)
- CEA: 4.2 ng/mL (Normal)
- AFP: 12 ng/mL (Normal)

Imaging Findings:
Ultrasound abdomen shows complex ovarian mass measuring 8.5 x 6.2 cm with solid and cystic components. Doppler flow demonstrates increased vascularity.

FINAL IMPRESSION:
Ovarian mass with elevated CA-125 suggestive of malignancy. Recommend further evaluation with CT scan and surgical consultation for definitive diagnosis and management.

Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
user_prompt:
Write one complete OPD Slip (Outpatient Department) for clinical domain General Medicine.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Maithili (code=mai, script=Devanagari). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match General Medicine — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Bihar, district=Araria.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Devanagari for Maithili.

PLACEMENT (OPD header): First line MUST include BOTH [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] (facility name) AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] (facility code) — never substitute one for the other.
Put [[EMPLOYEE_ID|…]] on the registrar/clerk line and [[RELATIVE_NAME|…]] on the attendant/relative line. Include [[DOB|YYYY-MM-DD]] and [[OCCUPATION|…]] with demographics.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 22
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Below Primary
- zone: Rural
- state: Bihar
- district: Araria
- first_language: Maithili
- clinical_persona_summary: A 22-year-old female from Araria, Bihar, currently married, with below-primary education and a rural occupation. Her first language is Maithili, with conversational Hindi proficiency. She follows a disciplined lifestyle with community engagement and traditional dietary habits.

Document format guide for OPD Slip (Outpatient Department) (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton (adapt language/script; keep tags):
OPD Slip | [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital X]] | ID [[HOSPITAL_ID|DH-X-01]]
Patient: [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] | DOB [[DOB|1992-03-14]] | Age: [[AGE|34]] | Gender: [[GENDER|Female]]
Occupation: [[OCCUPATION|Teacher]] | MRN: [[MRN|OPD-2024-001]] | Doctor: [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. ...]]
Relative: [[RELATIVE_NAME|...]] | Phone: [[PHONE_NUMBER|98XXXXXXXX]]
Registrar EmpID: [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-4412]] | District: [[DISTRICT|...]]
Chief complaint: <short clinical line matching assigned domain>

MANDATORY inline entities (13 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOB — Date of Birth e.g. [[DOB|1992-03-14]]
- OCCUPATION — Occupation e.g. [[OCCUPATION|Teacher]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- HOSPITAL_ID — Hospital ID e.g. [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- EMPLOYEE_ID — Employee ID e.g. [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-77821]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
generated_text:
OPD Slip | [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Araria District Hospital]] | ID [[HOSPITAL_ID|ADH-AR-001]]
Patient: [[PATIENT_NAME|Sita Kumari]] | DOB [[DOB|2002-07-22]] | Age: [[AGE|22]] | Gender: [[GENDER|Female]]
Occupation: [[OCCUPATION|Homemaker]] | MRN: [[MRN|OPD-2024-0815-042]] | Doctor: [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rajesh Kumar]]
Relative: [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ram Prasad Mandal]] | Phone: [[PHONE_NUMBER|9431023456]]
Registrar EmpID: [[EMPLOYEE_ID|REG-0089]] | District: [[DISTRICT|Araria]]
Chief complaint: Fever with headache and body ache for 3 days.
History of present illness: Patient reports onset of fever 3 days ago, associated with severe headache and generalized body ache. No chills or rigors. Fever is high-grade, around 102°F. She has been taking paracetamol from a local medical shop with partial relief. No history of similar episodes.
On examination: Patient is conscious and oriented. Temperature 101.8°F, Pulse 98/min, BP 120/80 mmHg, Respiratory rate 18/min. General appearance shows mild pallor. No signs of dehydration. Chest examination is clear bilaterally. Abdomen is soft and non-tender. No organomegaly.
Investigations: CBC shows mild leukocytosis. Widal test negative.
Diagnosis: Viral fever with myalgia.
Treatment: Paracetamol 500mg TDS for fever. Advise rest, adequate fluid intake, and a light diet.
Follow-up: Review after 3 days if symptoms persist.

professional_persona: Tetri Devi, an organized village event coordinator with a flair for Madhubani painting, channels their disciplined mindset and sociable energy into overseeing festivals and launching a home‑based art and pickles venture.
linguistic_persona: Tetri Devi, a native Maithili speaker with basic reading and writing skills, converses fluently in Hindi and picks up borrowed English words from television, though they cannot yet read or write English confidently.
sports_persona: Tetri Devi enjoys watching cricket on television, they cheer for the Bihar cricket team during domestic matches, and they occasionally participate in village kabaddi sessions at the community ground to stay fit.
arts_persona: Tetri Devi creates vibrant Madhubani paintings inspired by folk tales, they sing along to Maithili and Bhojpuri tracks by Sharda Sinha, and they collect handmade crafts from neighboring villages, weaving these artistic influences into their daily life.
travel_persona: Tetri Devi prefers short road trips to nearby towns like Patna and Darbhanga for market days, they dream of visiting Varanasi’s ghats and Delhi’s museums, and they plan to explore neighboring states during festival seasons when travel is affordable.
culinary_persona: Tetri Devi prepares traditional Maithili dishes such as litti‑chokha, fish curry with mustard seed paste, and dhuska, enjoys the aroma of cumin and fenugreek in their kitchen, and prefers dining at family gatherings where bharta and makhana are served.
persona: Tetri Devi, an imaginative 22‑year‑old village organizer who blends disciplined budgeting, vibrant Madhubani art, and community cheerleading into a lively, slightly impulsive yet purpose‑driven life, and their infectious enthusiasm keeps neighbours engaged.
cultural_background: Tetri hails from the Maithil community in Araria, a rural part of the Mithila region in Bihar. Her upbringing is rooted in the vibrant cultural fabric of Mithila, renowned for folk songs, Madhubani art, and seasonal festivals aligned with the agricultural calendar. Growing up in a joint family, she respects traditional customs while also embracing curiosity about new ideas and artistic expressions. Her sociable nature makes her an active participant in village gatherings, melas, weddings, and other community events, often taking a leading role in organizing and adding a touch of creativity to celebrations.
linguistic_background: Linguistic background: Tetri's mother tongue is Maithili, which she speaks fluently and can read and write at a basic level despite having completed less than primary schooling. She is also conversationally proficient in Hindi, the lingua franca of Bihar, and can understand spoken Hindi well, though her Devanagari reading and writing skills are rudimentary. Her exposure to English is minimal, limited to a handful of borrowed words from television and radio; she cannot read or write English with confidence.
skills_and_expertise: Skills and expertise: Tetri excels at managing household chores, preparing traditional Maithili dishes, and budgeting modest family expenditures. Her disciplined mindset makes her an effective organizer for festivals and family functions, ensuring everything runs smoothly. She possesses artistic talent in Madhubani painting, as well as practical skills in sewing, embroidery, and crafting simple handmade items. Her confident communication style also enables her to serve as a liaison in community affairs and coordinate collective activities.
skills_and_expertise_list: ['Household management', 'Traditional Maithili cooking', 'Basic budgeting', 'Event coordination for festivals', 'Madhubani painting', 'Sewing and embroidery', 'Community liaison', 'Basic literacy in Maithili and Hindi']
hobbies_and_interests: Hobbies and interests: Tetri enjoys listening to folk music, especially Maithili and Bhojpuri songs, and often sings along. She delights in creating Madhubani art and making small handicrafts, as well as tending to a modest kitchen garden. Reading devotional literature and watching television serials provide her relaxation. Her sociable personality drives her to attend village fairs, engage in community discussions, and organize get‑together gatherings with neighbors.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ['Folk singing', 'Madhubani painting', 'Handicraft making', 'Gardening', 'Reading devotional literature', 'Attending village fairs', 'Socializing with neighbors', 'Listening to radio/TV programs', 'Experimenting with new recipes']
career_goals_and_ambitions: Tetri aims to deepen her family's prosperity by mastering household finance management, pursuing adult literacy, and channeling her artistic flair into a small home‑based venture selling Madhubani paintings and traditional pickles. She also aspires to become a community health volunteer, leveraging her sociable nature to spread awareness about nutrition, hygiene, and women's empowerment among villagers.
persona_clinical_summary: A 22-year-old female from Araria, Bihar, currently married, with no formal occupation, reports below primary education. She resides in a rural zone and speaks Maithili as her first language, with conversational Hindi proficiency. Her lifestyle includes community involvement and traditional cultural practices.
system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):

  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +

  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.

Write one complete PHC Register Entry (Primary Health Centre) for clinical domain Maternal Health.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Maithili (code=mai, script=Devanagari). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Maternal Health — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Bihar, district=Araria.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Devanagari for Maithili.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 22
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Below Primary
- zone: Rural
- state: Bihar
- district: Araria
- first_language: Maithili
- clinical_persona_summary: A 22-year-old female from Araria, Bihar, currently married, with no formal occupation, reports below primary education. She resides in a rural zone and speaks Maithili as her first language, with conversational Hindi proficiency. Her lifestyle includes community involvement and traditional cultural practices.

Document format guide for PHC Register Entry (Primary Health Centre) (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
PHC register — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|PHC ...]] Village [[VILLAGE|...]] District [[DISTRICT|...]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|19]] [[GENDER|Female]] | brief entry line

[[PATIENT_NAME|Poonam Devi]] [[AGE|22]] [[GENDER|Female]] | Routine Antenatal Visit

Patient, a 22-year-old married homemaker from [[VILLAGE|Bansgaon]], presented for her second antenatal check-up. Chief complaint: Normal pregnancy, no complaints. On examination, her blood pressure was 110/70 mmHg, weight 52 kg, and pulse 80/min. Abdominal examination revealed a single live fetus with a fetal heart rate of 140 bpm in cephalic presentation.
