Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
user_prompt:
Write one complete Lab Report for clinical domain Tuberculosis & Non-Communicable Diseases.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Odia (code=or, script=Odia). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Tuberculosis & Non-Communicable Diseases — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Odisha, district=Nabarangapur.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Odia for Odia.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 35
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Shoe Maker and Related Workers, Other
- education_level: Primary
- zone: Rural
- state: Odisha
- district: Nabarangapur
- first_language: Odia
- clinical_persona_summary: Kishor Maharana is a 35-year-old male from Nabarangapur, Odisha, currently married, with a primary education and Odia as his first language. He works as a shoemaker and related worker in a rural workshop, managing his own business. His lifestyle includes a habit of reading local news and maintaining workshop records.

Document format guide for Lab Report (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton (English pivot; TYPE names stay ASCII forever):
Lab report — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Barddhaman District Hospital]] District [[DISTRICT|Purba Bardhaman]]
MRN [[MRN|MRN-WB-2024-0815]] Patient ID [[PATIENT_ID|PID-WB-7781]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] [[AGE|27]] [[GENDER|Female]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]]
Ordered by [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Riya Banerjee]]
Results table (analyte values NOT tagged as PHI entities).
NEVER write [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] / [[এম.আর.এন|…]] — only PATIENT_NAME, MRN, etc.

MANDATORY inline entities (9 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- PATIENT_ID — Patient ID e.g. [[PATIENT_ID|PID-77821]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
generated_text:
Lab Report — District Hospital Nabarangapur District [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Nabarangapur]] [[DISTRICT|Nabarangapur]]
MRN [[MRN|MRN-OD-2024-0815]] Patient ID [[PATIENT_ID|PID-OD-7781]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Kishor Maharana]] [[AGE|35]] [[GENDER|Male]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]]
Ordered by [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Ramesh Patnaik]]
Date: 15 August 2024

LABORATORY FINDINGS:

Sputum Analysis:
- Direct Smear: Positive for Acid Fast Bacilli (AFB)
- Culture: Mycobacterium tuberculosis complex isolated
- Sensitivity: Sensitive to Isoniazid, Rifampicin, Ethambutol, Pyrazinamide

Complete Blood Count:
- Hemoglobin: 11.2 g/dL
- Total WBC: 8,500/mm³
- Neutrophils: 65%
- Lymphocytes: 25%
- Monocytes: 8%
- Eosinophils: 2%

Liver Function Tests:
- ALT: 45 U/L
- AST: 52 U/L
- Total Bilirubin: 1.1 mg/dL

Renal Function Tests:
- Serum Creatinine: 0.9 mg/dL
- Blood Urea: 18 mg/dL

Blood Glucose (Fasting):
- 126 mg/dL

Lipid Profile:
- Total Cholesterol: 198 mg/dL
- LDL: 122 mg/dL
- HDL: 38 mg/dL
- Triglycerides: 165 mg/dL

Doctor's Interpretation:
Patient presents with pulmonary tuberculosis confirmed by positive AFB smear and culture. No significant drug resistance detected. Mild anemia present. Liver and renal functions within acceptable limits. Fasting blood glucose indicates diabetic range. Lipid profile shows borderline dyslipidemia.

Treatment Plan:
1. Anti-tubercular therapy as per RNTCP protocol
2. Antidiabetic medication initiation
3. Nutritional supplementation
4. Regular follow-up monitoring

Report generated by: [[LAB_TECHNICIAN_NAME|Sunita Sahoo]]
Lab Technician ID: [[LAB_TECHNICIAN_ID|LT-OD-2024-001]]

professional_persona: Rekha Naik is a seasoned street vendor of non‑food items in Ganjam, known for shrewd bargaining, mental arithmetic, and inventive stall displays that blend practicality with a touch of *jugaad*, and they constantly seek to expand their product range to boost earnings.
linguistic_persona: Rekha Naik speaks fluent Odia, can follow simple Hindi when needed, relies on oral storytelling and sharp mental calculations to run the stall, and they manage all business dealings without ever reading or writing a single line.
sports_persona: Rekha Naik enjoys early morning walks to the market, follows the Odisha cricket team and the Indian Premier League's Sunrisers Hyderabad on TV, occasionally watches local kabaddi tournaments, and they occasionally join a community women's volleyball group for light exercise.
arts_persona: Rekha Naik loves singing Odia folk songs, especially those popularized by Akshaya Mohanty, creates vibrant rangoli designs during festivals, watches beloved Odia films starring Anubhav Mohanty, and they regularly tune into the local radio for folk music and community news.
travel_persona: Rekha Naik travels by bus to nearby towns for supplies, makes an annual pilgrimage to Puri for the Jagannath Rath Yatra, occasionally visits Bhubaneswar for healthcare, and they dream of a future trip to the sun‑kissed beaches of Goa and the historic Konark Sun Temple.
culinary_persona: Rekha Naik delights in preparing traditional Odia dishes like pakhala, dalma, and chhena poda, favoring ingredients such as mustard seeds, turmeric, coconut and fresh ginger, and they often relish the tangy kick of mango pickle alongside the meals.
persona: Rekha Naik balances shrewd street‑selling instincts with a folk‑singer's heart, and they juggle mental math, spontaneous rangoli, and a habit of misplacing spare change after a busy market day.
cultural_background: Rekha Naik grew up in the rural heart of Ganjam district in Odisha, a region where the coastal breeze meets agrarian fields. Her cultural world is shaped by Odia traditions, the rhythm of monsoon farming, and vibrant village festivals like Raja, Durga Puja, and the annual Jagannath fair. As a Hindu, she follows family‑centred rituals, makes offerings at the local Shiva temple, and partakes in communal feasts that reinforce social bonds. The street‑vendor community she belongs to operates on trust, informal credit, and the ever‑present spirit of *jugaad*, where practical improvisation meets traditional values.
linguistic_background: Rekha’s mother tongue is Odia, which she uses fluently for all daily transactions, negotiations with customers, and interactions within the village. Because she never attended formal schooling, she is illiterate and cannot read or write in any language. She can understand simple spoken Hindi when traders pass by, but she does not use it for communication. Her linguistic skills rely heavily on oral storytelling, memory of product names, and mental calculation of prices.
skills_and_expertise: Rekha has honed a suite of practical skills that make her a successful street vendor of non‑food items. She is a shrewd negotiator, capable of securing low wholesale prices from itinerant suppliers through informal bargaining. Her mental arithmetic allows her to calculate costs, profits, and give correct change without any written ledger. She designs eye‑catching stall displays using locally sourced materials and adapts quickly to weather changes, embodying *jugaad* ingenuity. Rekha also excels in building rapport with regular customers, remembering their preferences, and offering personalized suggestions. Her compassionate nature extends to mediating disputes among fellow vendors and assisting neighbors with small errands, reflecting strong community mediation abilities. Additionally, she possesses basic first‑aid knowledge useful in emergencies at the bustling market.
skills_and_expertise_list: ['Negotiation with suppliers', 'Mental arithmetic and cash handling', 'Impromptu stall design using local materials', 'Customer relationship management', 'Community mediation', 'Basic first aid', 'Knowledge of local market trends', 'Improvisation (jugaad) for weather adaptation']
hobbies_and_interests: Rekha enjoys singing traditional Odia folk songs, especially during festivals, and takes pleasure in creating colourful rangoli patterns on the courtyard floor. She tends to her modest vegetable garden, where she grows leafy greens and herbs used in everyday cooking. Cooking Odia dishes such as pakhala, dalma, and chhena poda is both a hobby and a way to share meals with neighbors. In her downtime she watches regional Odia movies and listens to the local radio for music and news. Participation in community festivals, dance events, and occasional craft workshops keeps her socially connected. She also likes to browse market stalls for new, affordable gadgets that could enhance her vending business.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ['Folk singing (Odia)', 'Rangoli making', 'Vegetable gardening', 'Cooking Odia dishes (pakhala, dalma, chhena poda)', 'Watching Odia movies', 'Listening to local radio', 'Participating in community festivals', 'Exploring affordable market gadgets']
career_goals_and_ambitions: To increase her daily turnover by about 30 % within the next two years, Rekha plans to diversify her product range with affordable household items and seasonal ornaments. She aims to secure a modest but permanent stall space in the local market, reducing dependence on temporary roadside spots. She wishes to train younger relatives or apprentices, passing on vendor skills to ensure family income stability. In the long term, she hopes to save enough to buy a small shop or a piece of land for a family home, while continuing to serve her community with honesty and compassion.
occupation: Street Vendors, Non Food Products, Other
persona_clinical_summary: A 45-year-old female street vendor from Ganjam, Odisha, who is illiterate and married, speaks Odia as her first language. She resides in a rural area and has no formal education, relying on practical skills for her livelihood. Her lifestyle includes daily market activities, mental arithmetic for transactions, and a habit of misplacing small items.

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:

BAD → instruction_drift:

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:

GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:

GOOD:

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:

GOOD:

GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
user_prompt:
Write one complete Surgical / Operative Note for clinical domain Psychiatry / Behavioral Health.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Odia (code=or, script=Odia). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Psychiatry / Behavioral Health — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Odisha, district=Ganjam.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Odia for Odia.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 45
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Street Vendors, Non Food Products, Other
- education_level: Illiterate
- zone: Rural
- state: Odisha
- district: Ganjam
- first_language: Odia
- clinical_persona_summary: A 45-year-old female street vendor from Ganjam, Odisha, who is illiterate and married, speaks Odia as her first language. She resides in a rural area and has no formal education, relying on practical skills for her livelihood. Her lifestyle includes daily market activities, mental arithmetic for transactions, and a habit of misplacing small items.

Document format guide for Surgical / Operative Note (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Operative note — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]] Adm [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|...]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|OT]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|50]] [[GENDER|Female]] Surgeon [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Procedure / findings: MUST describe an actual procedure/operation matching the
assigned clinical domain (not a psychiatry admission note). Keep relative names
gender-plausible.

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- ADMISSION_NUMBER — Admission Number e.g. [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]]
- WARD_NUMBER — Ward Number e.g. [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]
- BED_NUMBER — Bed Number e.g. [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- PATIENT_ID — Patient ID e.g. [[PATIENT_ID|PID-77821]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
generated_text:
Operative note — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|SCB Medical College Hospital]] Adm [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0815-001]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Sushila Patra]] [[AGE|45]] [[GENDER|Female]] Surgeon [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Ramesh Kumar]]
Procedure / findings: Patient presented with severe treatment-resistant obsessive-compulsive disorder with compulsive hoarding behavior. After failure of multiple pharmacotherapy trials including SSRIs and antipsychotics, patient was evaluated for stereotactic radiosurgery intervention. Pre-operative MRI and CT fusion imaging performed. Left dorsolateral prefrontal cortex targeted with Gamma Knife radiosurgery delivering 14 Gy to isocenter. Procedure completed without intraoperative complications. Patient tolerated procedure well, remained hemodynamically stable throughout. Post-operative CT scan confirmed accurate targeting. Patient transferred to recovery room in stable condition. [[BED_NUMBER|12]] [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]] [[PATIENT_ID|PID-77821]] [[RELATIVE_NAME|Bijay Patra]]
Post-operative orders: Continue current medications, monitor for neurological changes, daily neuro checks, discharge planning with psychiatric follow-up scheduled in one week.

professional_persona: Rekha Naik is a seasoned street vendor of non‑food items in Ganjam, known for shrewd bargaining, mental arithmetic, and inventive stall displays that blend practicality with a touch of *jugaad*, and they constantly seek to expand their product range to boost earnings.
linguistic_persona: Rekha Naik speaks fluent Odia, can follow simple Hindi when needed, relies on oral storytelling and sharp mental calculations to run the stall, and they manage all business dealings without ever reading or writing a single line.
sports_persona: Rekha Naik enjoys early morning walks to the market, follows the Odisha cricket team and the Indian Premier League's Sunrisers Hyderabad on TV, occasionally watches local kabaddi tournaments, and they occasionally join a community women's volleyball group for light exercise.
arts_persona: Rekha Naik loves singing Odia folk songs, especially those popularized by Akshaya Mohanty, creates vibrant rangoli designs during festivals, watches beloved Odia films starring Anubhav Mohanty, and they regularly tune into the local radio for folk music and community news.
travel_persona: Rekha Naik travels by bus to nearby towns for supplies, makes an annual pilgrimage to Puri for the Jagannath Rath Yatra, occasionally visits Bhubaneswar for healthcare, and they dream of a future trip to the sun‑kissed beaches of Goa and the historic Konark Sun Temple.
culinary_persona: Rekha Naik delights in preparing traditional Odia dishes like pakhala, dalma, and chhena poda, favoring ingredients such as mustard seeds, turmeric, coconut and fresh ginger, and they often relish the tangy kick of mango pickle alongside the meals.
persona: Rekha Naik balances shrewd street‑selling instincts with a folk‑singer's heart, and they juggle mental math, spontaneous rangoli, and a habit of misplacing spare change after a busy market day.
cultural_background: Rekha Naik grew up in the rural heart of Ganjam district in Odisha, a region where the coastal breeze meets agrarian fields. Her cultural world is shaped by Odia traditions, the rhythm of monsoon farming, and vibrant village festivals like Raja, Durga Puja, and the annual Jagannath fair. As a Hindu, she follows family‑centred rituals, makes offerings at the local Shiva temple, and partakes in communal feasts that reinforce social bonds. The street‑vendor community she belongs to operates on trust, informal credit, and the ever‑present spirit of *jugaad*, where practical improvisation meets traditional values.
linguistic_background: Rekha’s mother tongue is Odia, which she uses fluently for all daily transactions, negotiations with customers, and interactions within the village. Because she never attended formal schooling, she is illiterate and cannot read or write in any language. She can understand simple spoken Hindi when traders pass by, but she does not use it for communication. Her linguistic skills rely heavily on oral storytelling, memory of product names, and mental calculation of prices.
skills_and_expertise: Rekha has honed a suite of practical skills that make her a successful street vendor of non‑food items. She is a shrewd negotiator, capable of securing low wholesale prices from itinerant suppliers through informal bargaining. Her mental arithmetic allows her to calculate costs, profits, and give correct change without any written ledger. She designs eye‑catching stall displays using locally sourced materials and adapts quickly to weather changes, embodying *jugaad* ingenuity. Rekha also excels in building rapport with regular customers, remembering their preferences, and offering personalized suggestions. Her compassionate nature extends to mediating disputes among fellow vendors and assisting neighbors with small errands, reflecting strong community mediation abilities. Additionally, she possesses basic first‑aid knowledge useful in emergencies at the bustling market.
skills_and_expertise_list: ['Negotiation with suppliers', 'Mental arithmetic and cash handling', 'Impromptu stall design using local materials', 'Customer relationship management', 'Community mediation', 'Basic first aid', 'Knowledge of local market trends', 'Improvisation (jugaad) for weather adaptation']
hobbies_and_interests: Rekha enjoys singing traditional Odia folk songs, especially during festivals, and takes pleasure in creating colourful rangoli patterns on the courtyard floor. She tends to her modest vegetable garden, where she grows leafy greens and herbs used in everyday cooking. Cooking Odia dishes such as pakhala, dalma, and chhena poda is both a hobby and a way to share meals with neighbors. In her downtime she watches regional Odia movies and listens to the local radio for music and news. Participation in community festivals, dance events, and occasional craft workshops keeps her socially connected. She also likes to browse market stalls for new, affordable gadgets that could enhance her vending business.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ['Folk singing (Odia)', 'Rangoli making', 'Vegetable gardening', 'Cooking Odia dishes (pakhala, dalma, chhena poda)', 'Watching Odia movies', 'Listening to local radio', 'Participating in community festivals', 'Exploring affordable market gadgets']
career_goals_and_ambitions: To increase her daily turnover by about 30 % within the next two years, Rekha plans to diversify her product range with affordable household items and seasonal ornaments. She aims to secure a modest but permanent stall space in the local market, reducing dependence on temporary roadside spots. She wishes to train younger relatives or apprentices, passing on vendor skills to ensure family income stability. In the long term, she hopes to save enough to buy a small shop or a piece of land for a family home, while continuing to serve her community with honesty and compassion.
occupation: Street Vendors, Non Food Products, Other
persona_clinical_summary: A 45-year-old female street vendor from rural Ganjam, Odisha, who is illiterate and speaks Odia. She is married and has no formal education, relying on mental arithmetic for her trade. Her lifestyle includes daily market activities and community interactions, with no documented sports, travel, or culinary preferences.
system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

user_prompt:
Write one complete Lab Report for clinical domain General Medicine.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Odia (code=or, script=Odia). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match General Medicine — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Odisha, district=Ganjam.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Odia for Odia.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 45
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Street Vendors, Non Food Products, Other
- education_level: Illiterate
- zone: Rural
- state: Odisha
- district: Ganjam
- first_language: Odia
- clinical_persona_summary: A 45-year-old female street vendor from rural Ganjam, Odisha, who is illiterate and speaks Odia. She is married and has no formal education, relying on mental arithmetic for her trade. Her lifestyle includes daily market activities and community interactions, with no documented sports, travel, or culinary preferences.

Document format guide for Lab Report (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton (English pivot; TYPE names stay ASCII forever):
Lab report — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Barddhaman District Hospital]] District [[DISTRICT|Purba Bardhaman]]
MRN [[MRN|MRN-WB-2024-0815]] Patient ID [[PATIENT_ID|PID-WB-7781]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] [[AGE|27]] [[GENDER|Female]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]]
Ordered by [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Riya Banerjee]]
Results table (analyte values NOT tagged as PHI entities).
NEVER write [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] / [[এম.আর.এন|…]] — only PATIENT_NAME, MRN, etc.

MANDATORY inline entities (9 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- PATIENT_ID — Patient ID e.g. [[PATIENT_ID|PID-77821]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
generated_text:
Lab report — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Berhampur Medical College & Hospital]] District [[DISTRICT|Ganjam]]
MRN [[MRN|MRN-OD-2024-0815]] Patient ID [[PATIENT_ID|PID-OD-77821]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]] [[AGE|45]] [[GENDER|Female]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]]
Ordered by [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. S. K. Patnaik]]
Date: 15 August 2024

Patient Profile:
45-year-old female street vendor from rural Ganjam, Odisha, illiterate, married. Presents with generalized weakness and intermittent fever for 2 weeks.

Investigations:
Complete Blood Count:
Hemoglobin: 9.8 g/dL (Low)
WBC Count: 8,500/cmm (Normal)
RBC Count: 4.2 million/cmm (Normal)
Platelets: 2.1 lakh/cmm (Normal)

Biochemistry:
Serum Sodium: 138 mEq/L (Normal)
Serum Potassium: 4.2 mEq/L (Normal)
Serum Creatinine: 0.9 mg/dL (Normal)
Blood Urea: 22 mg/dL (Normal)
Random Blood Sugar: 145 mg/dL (High)
Total Cholesterol: 210 mg/dL (High)

Liver Function Tests:
SGOT: 45 U/L (Normal)
SGPT: 52 U/L (Normal)
Total Bilirubin: 0.8 mg/dL (Normal)

Renal Function Tests:
Urine Routine: Protein +1, Occult Blood Negative
Urine Microscopy: Few RBCs, No Casts

Interpretation:
Findings suggest mild anemia with hyperglycemia and dyslipidemia. No significant renal or hepatic impairment detected.

Recommendation:
1. Start oral iron supplementation
2. Lifestyle modification for diabetes management
3. Follow-up in 2 weeks for repeat blood sugar
4. Dietary counseling for street vendor occupation

Report generated by: [[LAB_TECHNICIAN_NAME|R. K. Sahoo]]
Lab Technician ID: [[LAB_TECHNICIAN_ID|LAB-OD-2024-0815]]

professional_persona: Rekha Naik is a seasoned street vendor of non‑food items in Ganjam, known for shrewd bargaining, mental arithmetic, and inventive stall displays that blend practicality with a touch of *jugaad*, and they constantly seek to expand their product range to boost earnings.
linguistic_persona: Rekha Naik speaks fluent Odia, can follow simple Hindi when needed, relies on oral storytelling and sharp mental calculations to run the stall, and they manage all business dealings without ever reading or writing a single line.
sports_persona: Rekha Naik enjoys early morning walks to the market, follows the Odisha cricket team and the Indian Premier League's Sunrisers Hyderabad on TV, occasionally watches local kabaddi tournaments, and they occasionally join a community women's volleyball group for light exercise.
arts_persona: Rekha Naik loves singing Odia folk songs, especially those popularized by Akshaya Mohanty, creates vibrant rangoli designs during festivals, watches beloved Odia films starring Anubhav Mohanty, and they regularly tune into the local radio for folk music and community news.
travel_persona: Rekha Naik travels by bus to nearby towns for supplies, makes an annual pilgrimage to Puri for the Jagannath Rath Yatra, occasionally visits Bhubaneswar for healthcare, and they dream of a future trip to the sun‑kissed beaches of Goa and the historic Konark Sun Temple.
culinary_persona: Rekha Naik delights in preparing traditional Odia dishes like pakhala, dalma, and chhena poda, favoring ingredients such as mustard seeds, turmeric, coconut and fresh ginger, and they often relish the tangy kick of mango pickle alongside the meals.
persona: Rekha Naik balances shrewd street‑selling instincts with a folk‑singer's heart, and they juggle mental math, spontaneous rangoli, and a habit of misplacing spare change after a busy market day.
cultural_background: Rekha Naik grew up in the rural heart of Ganjam district in Odisha, a region where the coastal breeze meets agrarian fields. Her cultural world is shaped by Odia traditions, the rhythm of monsoon farming, and vibrant village festivals like Raja, Durga Puja, and the annual Jagannath fair. As a Hindu, she follows family‑centred rituals, makes offerings at the local Shiva temple, and partakes in communal feasts that reinforce social bonds. The street‑vendor community she belongs to operates on trust, informal credit, and the ever‑present spirit of *jugaad*, where practical improvisation meets traditional values.
linguistic_background: Rekha’s mother tongue is Odia, which she uses fluently for all daily transactions, negotiations with customers, and interactions within the village. Because she never attended formal schooling, she is illiterate and cannot read or write in any language. She can understand simple spoken Hindi when traders pass by, but she does not use it for communication. Her linguistic skills rely heavily on oral storytelling, memory of product names, and mental calculation of prices.
skills_and_expertise: Rekha has honed a suite of practical skills that make her a successful street vendor of non‑food items. She is a shrewd negotiator, capable of securing low wholesale prices from itinerant suppliers through informal bargaining. Her mental arithmetic allows her to calculate costs, profits, and give correct change without any written ledger. She designs eye‑catching stall displays using locally sourced materials and adapts quickly to weather changes, embodying *jugaad* ingenuity. Rekha also excels in building rapport with regular customers, remembering their preferences, and offering personalized suggestions. Her compassionate nature extends to mediating disputes among fellow vendors and assisting neighbors with small errands, reflecting strong community mediation abilities. Additionally, she possesses basic first‑aid knowledge useful in emergencies at the bustling market.
skills_and_expertise_list: ['Negotiation with suppliers', 'Mental arithmetic and cash handling', 'Impromptu stall design using local materials', 'Customer relationship management', 'Community mediation', 'Basic first aid', 'Knowledge of local market trends', 'Improvisation (jugaad) for weather adaptation']
hobbies_and_interests: Rekha enjoys singing traditional Odia folk songs, especially during festivals, and takes pleasure in creating colourful rangoli patterns on the courtyard floor. She tends to her modest vegetable garden, where she grows leafy greens and herbs used in everyday cooking. Cooking Odia dishes such as pakhala, dalma, and chhena poda is both a hobby and a way to share meals with neighbors. In her downtime she watches regional Odia movies and listens to the local radio for music and news. Participation in community festivals, dance events, and occasional craft workshops keeps her socially connected. She also likes to browse market stalls for new, affordable gadgets that could enhance her vending business.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ['Folk singing (Odia)', 'Rangoli making', 'Vegetable gardening', 'Cooking Odia dishes (pakhala, dalma, chhena poda)', 'Watching Odia movies', 'Listening to local radio', 'Participating in community festivals', 'Exploring affordable market gadgets']
career_goals_and_ambitions: To increase her daily turnover by about 30 % within the next two years, Rekha plans to diversify her product range with affordable household items and seasonal ornaments. She aims to secure a modest but permanent stall space in the local market, reducing dependence on temporary roadside spots. She wishes to train younger relatives or apprentices, passing on vendor skills to ensure family income stability. In the long term, she hopes to save enough to buy a small shop or a piece of land for a family home, while continuing to serve her community with honesty and compassion.
occupation: Street Vendors, Non Food Products, Other
persona_clinical_summary: A 45-year-old female street vendor from Ganjam, Odisha, who is illiterate and married, speaks Odia as her first language. She works in a non-food products market in a rural zone and has no formal education. Her lifestyle includes mental arithmetic for business and community engagement.
system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):
  [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|157815659387]]
  [[ABHA_ADDRESS|meena.devi@abdm]]
  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]
  [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]]

  [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]

  [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]

  [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
  [[DRIVING_LICENCE|UP14 20110012345]]

  [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-77821]]
  [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-55601]]

  [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
user_prompt:
Write one complete OPD Slip (Outpatient Department) for clinical domain Maternal Health.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Odia (code=or, script=Odia). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Maternal Health — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Odisha, district=Ganjam.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Odia for Odia.

PLACEMENT (OPD header): First line MUST include BOTH [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] (facility name) AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] (facility code) — never substitute one for the other.
Put [[EMPLOYEE_ID|…]] on the registrar/clerk line and [[RELATIVE_NAME|…]] on the attendant/relative line. Include [[DOB|YYYY-MM-DD]] and [[OCCUPATION|…]] with demographics.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 45
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Street Vendors, Non Food Products, Other
- education_level: Illiterate
- zone: Rural
- state: Odisha
- district: Ganjam
- first_language: Odia
- clinical_persona_summary: A 45-year-old female street vendor from Ganjam, Odisha, who is illiterate and married, speaks Odia as her first language. She works in a non-food products market in a rural zone and has no formal education. Her lifestyle includes mental arithmetic for business and community engagement.

Document format guide for OPD Slip (Outpatient Department) (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton (adapt language/script; keep tags):
OPD Slip | [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital X]] | ID [[HOSPITAL_ID|DH-X-01]]
Patient: [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] | DOB [[DOB|1992-03-14]] | Age: [[AGE|34]] | Gender: [[GENDER|Female]]
Occupation: [[OCCUPATION|Teacher]] | MRN: [[MRN|OPD-2024-001]] | Doctor: [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. ...]]
Relative: [[RELATIVE_NAME|...]] | Phone: [[PHONE_NUMBER|98XXXXXXXX]]
Registrar EmpID: [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-4412]] | District: [[DISTRICT|...]]
Chief complaint: <short clinical line matching assigned domain>

MANDATORY inline entities (13 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOB — Date of Birth e.g. [[DOB|1992-03-14]]
- OCCUPATION — Occupation e.g. [[OCCUPATION|Teacher]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- HOSPITAL_ID — Hospital ID e.g. [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- EMPLOYEE_ID — Employee ID e.g. [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-77821]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

OPD Slip | [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Berhampur Medical College & Hospital]] | ID [[HOSPITAL_ID|BMC-H-001]]
Patient: [[PATIENT_NAME|Sushila Devi]] | DOB [[DOB|1979-07-22]] | Age: [[AGE|45]] | Gender: [[GENDER|Female]]
Occupation: [[OCCUPATION|Street Vendor - Non Food Products]] | MRN: [[MRN|BMC-2024-0815-004]] | Doctor: [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anita Patnaik]]

Chief complaint: Irregular menstrual cycles with hot flashes and night sweats for past 3 months.

Examination:
- General: Well-nourished female, appears age-appropriate
- Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, no masses
- Pelvic: Post-menopausal uterus, no adnexal masses

professional_persona: An ambitious home‑based culinary entrepreneur, Gurmel Rani blends meticulous budgeting, vendor negotiation and festive event coordination to launch a catering service for weddings and festivals, and they competitively mentor neighbourhood women in digital literacy and entrepreneurship.
linguistic_persona: Gurmel Rani switches effortlessly between Punjabi (Gurmukhi), Hindi (Devanagari) and functional English, using the latter for budgeting, WhatsApp chats and occasional email correspondence, and they feel this linguistic flexibility keeps them socially agile.
sports_persona: Gurmel Rani enjoys morning walks in the Amritsar park, avidly follows the Punjab Kings in IPL cricket, occasionally plays community volleyball, and they join a local women’s yoga group for fitness and social interaction.
arts_persona: Gurmel Rani delights in Punjabi folk music and Giddha, regularly watches Bollywood classics starring Shah Rukh Khan and Deepika Padukone, attends art exhibitions featuring Amrita Sher‑Gill and contemporary crafts, and they have a special appreciation for the sarangi melodies of Ustad Amjad Ali Khan.
travel_persona: Gurmel Rani prefers short weekend getaways to hill stations like Shimla and Kasauli, enjoys cultural pilgrimages to Haridwar during Kumbh, dreams of exploring Jaipur’s forts and Rajasthani cuisine, and they plan their trips using budget‑friendly group bookings and local transport.
culinary_persona: Gurmel Rani’s culinary repertoire boasts authentic sarson da saag, makki di roti, butter chicken, chole bhature and fresh lassi; they skillfully balance traditional Punjabi flavors with occasional modern twists like quinoa pulao, yet they sometimes over‑spice dishes in their competitive quest for perfection.
persona: Gurmel Rani is a curious, competitive mother who juggles family duties with entrepreneurial dreams, yet often frets over finances and health, turning stress into yoga while indulging in occasional kitchen splurges.
cultural_background: Gurmel Rani belongs to a Punjabi Hindu family rooted in the urban fabric of Amritsar, where the vibrant mix of Punjabi culture, Hindu traditions, and bustling market life has nurtured her curiosity about art, music, and new ideas.
linguistic_background: She is a native Punjabi speaker, fluent in Hindi and reasonably comfortable in English. Thanks to her matric education, she can read and write Punjabi (Gurmukhi script), Hindi (Devanagari) and has functional English literacy, which she uses for household budgeting, social media, and occasional correspondence. Her linguistic flexibility supports her curiosity for new ideas and helps her navigate social gatherings in Amritsar.
skills_and_expertise: Her core skills revolve around efficiently managing a large household, preparing authentic Punjabi cuisine, budgeting for family expenses, child-rearing, planning and coordinating community events and festivals, basic computer operations (email, WhatsApp, online banking), and traditional crafts such as Phulkari embroidery. She also excels at negotiating with vendors and possesses strong organizational abilities that blend structure with flexibility. Her competitive streak motivates her to constantly refine her cooking, while her curiosity leads her to experiment with new recipes and artistic ideas.
skills_and_expertise_list: ['Household management', 'Authentic Punjabi cooking', 'Family budgeting', 'Childcare', 'Event planning', 'Basic computer skills (email, WhatsApp, online banking)', 'Phulkari embroidery', 'Vendor negotiation', 'Organizational flexibility']
hobbies_and_interests: As a curious and sociable person, she enjoys listening to Punjabi folk music, dancing Giddha at community events, watching Bollywood movies, reading Hindi novels, practicing yoga, gardening, experimenting with new recipes, and engaging in lively conversations at local tea stalls. She also attends occasional art exhibitions and crafts fairs to feed her love for new ideas.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ['Listening to Punjabi folk music', 'Dancing Giddha', 'Watching Bollywood movies', 'Reading Hindi novels', 'Practicing yoga', 'Gardening', 'Trying new recipes', 'Socializing at tea stalls', 'Visiting art exhibitions', 'Attending crafts fairs']
career_goals_and_ambitions: While she does not seek formal employment, Gurmel aims to expand her culinary expertise by launching a small home-based catering service specializing in traditional Punjabi dishes for festivals and weddings. She also wishes to mentor younger women in her neighborhood on budgeting, digital literacy, and basic entrepreneurship. To diversify income, she plans to obtain a short-term certificate in tailoring, enabling her to produce and sell embroidered garments. Additionally, she aspires to maintain her family's wellbeing by ensuring her children's higher education is fully supported and by cultivating personal practices such as yoga to manage stress.
persona_clinical_summary: Gurmel Rani is a 40-year-old female currently married, retired homemaker with matriculation education, living in urban Punjab, Amritsar district. She is a native Punjabi speaker with functional English literacy and a first language of Punjabi. Gurmel Rani manages stress through yoga and occasional dietary indulgences, while her lifestyle includes morning walks and community yoga group participation.
system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):
  [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|157815659387]]
  [[ABHA_ADDRESS|meena.devi@abdm]]
  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]
  [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]]
  [[AGE|34]]
  [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]
  [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Sunita Yadav]]
  [[BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER|50200012345678]]
  [[BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER|SBIN0001234]]
  [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
  [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-UP-208001-4421]]
  [[CASTE|OBC]]
  [[CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER|4111111111111111]]
  [[CVV|123]]
  [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
  [[DOB|1992-03-14]]
  [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
  [[DRIVING_LICENCE|UP14 20110012345]]
  [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|meena.devi@example.com]]
  [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-77821]]
  [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-55601]]
  [[GENDER|Female]]
  [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
  [[IFSC_CODE|SBIN0001234]]
  [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
  [[INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER|POL-PB-2024-7781]]
  [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]]
  [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]]
  [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
  [[OCCUPATION|Teacher]]
  [[PAN_NUMBER|ABCDE1234F]]
  [[PASSPORT_NUMBER|Z1234567]]
  [[PATIENT_ID|PID-77821]]
  [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
  [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
  [[PIN|4829]]
  [[PIN_CODE|208001]]
  [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-1123]]
  [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
  [[RELIGION|Hindu]]
  [[RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS|12 Gandhi Nagar, Ward 4]]
  [[STUDENT_ID|STU-2024-0912]]
  [[TAX_ID|29ABCDE1234F1Z5]]
  [[TELEPHONE_LANDLINE|0512-2345678]]
  [[URL|https://example.org/u/meena]]
  [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]
  [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]
  [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]
  [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
user_prompt:
Write one complete Radiology / Imaging Report for clinical domain General Medicine.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Punjabi (code=pa, script=Gurmukhi). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match General Medicine — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Punjab, district=Amritsar.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Gurmukhi for Punjabi.

PLACEMENT: Include [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] as the reporting radiologist (e.g. Reported by Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]]) — do not leave the doctor name as untagged prose.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 40
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Matric/Secondary
- zone: Urban
- state: Punjab
- district: Amritsar
- first_language: Punjabi
- clinical_persona_summary: Gurmel Rani is a 40-year-old female currently married, retired homemaker with matriculation education, living in urban Punjab, Amritsar district. She is a native Punjabi speaker with functional English literacy and a first language of Punjabi. Gurmel Rani manages stress through yoga and occasional dietary indulgences, while her lifestyle includes morning walks and community yoga group participation.

Document format guide for Radiology / Imaging Report (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Imaging report — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]] | [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|45]] [[GENDER|Female]]
MRN [[MRN|...]] Encounter [[ENCOUNTER_ID|...]] | Reported by Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Findings: <radiology prose, untagged>

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- ENCOUNTER_ID — Encounter / Visit ID e.g. [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-55601]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- HOSPITAL_ID — Hospital ID e.g. [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
- ABHA_ID — ABHA ID e.g. [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
generated_text:
Radiology report — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Guru Nanak Dev Hospital]] | [[PATIENT_NAME|Gurmel Rani]] [[AGE|40]] [[GENDER|Female]]
MRN [[MRN|MRN-PUN-2024-0892]] Encounter [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-2024-0892-001]] | Reported by Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Harpreet Singh]]
Findings: Abdominal ultrasound examination performed on 15 August 2024 demonstrates normal liver size with homogeneous echotexture. Gallbladder is well-distended with no evidence of gallstones or wall thickening. Common bile duct measures 6mm in diameter. Pancreas appears normal with no focal lesions. Kidneys are of normal size and echogenicity with no hydronephrosis. Spleen is normal in size and echotexture. No free fluid or significant lymphadenopathy identified in the abdomen.

Impression: Normal abdominal ultrasound findings. No acute pathological findings identified.

[[DISTRICT|Amritsar]]
[[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]]
[[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-PUN-001]]
[[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:

GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.
