Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
user_prompt:
Write one complete Insurance Claim / TPA Form for clinical domain General Medicine.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Telugu (code=te, script=Telugu). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match General Medicine — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Telangana, district=Mahbubnagar.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Telugu for Telugu.

PLACEMENT (vehicle): Put VEHICLE_REGISTRATION exactly once (parking/ambulance or motor claim line). Do not dump or repeat it.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 60
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Matric/Secondary
- zone: Urban
- state: Telangana
- district: Mahbubnagar
- first_language: Telugu
- clinical_persona_summary: A 60-year-old male, currently married, retired, with a matriculation education, residing in urban Telangana. He is a native Telugu speaker with limited Hindi/English proficiency. His lifestyle includes daily morning walks, light yoga, and solitary low-impact exercise, while neglecting some health routines. He follows Hindu traditions and celebrates regional festivals.

Document format guide for Insurance Claim / TPA Form (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
TPA claim — Policy [[INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER|POL-PB-2024-7781]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|25]] [[GENDER|Male]] Aadhaar [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|157815659387]]
Hospital [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]] District [[DISTRICT|...]]
Motor / RTA vehicle [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|PB10AB1234]] (exactly once).
PAN [[PAN_NUMBER|ABCDE1234F]] IFSC [[IFSC_CODE|SBIN0001234]] account [[BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER|...]]

MANDATORY inline entities (16 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER — Insurance Policy Number e.g. [[INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER|POL-PB-2024-7781]]
- AADHAAR_NUMBER — Aadhaar Number e.g. [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|157815659387]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- PAN_NUMBER — PAN Number e.g. [[PAN_NUMBER|ABCDE1234F]]
- BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER — Bank Account Number e.g. [[BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER|50200012345678]]
- IFSC_CODE — IFSC Code e.g. [[IFSC_CODE|SBIN0001234]]
- BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER — Bank Routing Number e.g. [[BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER|SBIN0001234]]
- CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER — Credit Card Number e.g. [[CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER|4111111111111111]]
- CVV — CVV e.g. [[CVV|123]]
- PIN — PIN e.g. [[PIN|4829]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION — Vehicle Registration Number e.g. [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
generated_text:
TPA Claim Form — Policy [[INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER|POL-TG-2024-5566]]
Patient Details
[[PATIENT_NAME|Ramesh Reddy]] [[AGE|60]] [[GENDER|Male]] Aadhaar [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|890123456789]]
[[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]] [[PIN|502201]]
Policy Holder Details
PAN [[PAN_NUMBER|ABCDR1234F]] IFSC [[IFSC_CODE|SBIN0000678]] account [[BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER|678901234567]]
Bank Routing Number [[BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER|SBIN0000678]]
Credit Card [[CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER|4111111111111111]] CVV [[CVV|123]]
Hospital Details
Hospital [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Mahbubnagar District Hospital]] District [[DISTRICT|Mahbubnagar]]
Claim Details
Date of Admission: 15th November 2024
Date of Discharge: 20th November 2024
Mode of Admission: Emergency via Ambulance [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|TS09AB1234]]
Clinical Summary
Patient, a 60-year-old male, presented to the emergency department with a chief complaint of severe headache, chest pain, and shortness of breath for the past 6 hours. He is a known case of hypertension and type 2 diabetes mellitus, admitted for hypertensive emergency. On examination, the patient was conscious but in distress. Vital signs were BP 180/110 mmHg, HR 105 bpm, RR 24/min, SpO2 92% on room air. ECG showed ST-segment elevation in leads V2-V4, and cardiac enzymes were elevated. The patient was diagnosed with hypertensive emergency with acute coronary syndrome. He was managed in the cardiac care unit with IV antihypertensives, antiplatelet therapy, and anticoagulation. His condition stabilized, and he was discharged on the 5th day with a revised medication regimen and follow-up instructions.

professional_persona: Sudheera Bachiya, a retired homemaker and community storyteller, masterfully blends expertise in traditional regional cooking, hand‑loom weaving, and village event coordination with their vivid imagination, and they keep local folklore thriving.
linguistic_persona: Sudheera Bachiya, an illiterate yet verbally fluent elder, converses effortlessly in Urdu, understands Hindi and Bhojpuri enough to join village conversations, and they draw creative inspiration from listening to ghazals despite never having learned to read.
sports_persona: Sudheera Bachiya, an enthusiastic spectator of village cricket matches who roots for the Mumbai Indians, enjoys occasional yoga stretches in the courtyard, and they join the local dance circle during Holi, though they prefer gentle walks over rigorous sports.
arts_persona: Sudheera Bachiya, a passionate lover of Urdu ghazals, folk songs by Sharda Sinha, and vibrant rangoli art, often hums Kishore Kumar while sketching simple floral designs during festivals, and they use their storytelling flair to bring these artistic expressions to life.
travel_persona: Sudheera Bachiya, who enjoys modest trips to nearby towns like Patna and Varanasi for temple fairs and fresh spices, dreams of a winter pilgrimage to Rishikesh, and they plan journeys with flexibility, relying on relatives for transport and avoiding strict itineraries.
culinary_persona: Sudheera Bachiya, a master of traditional regional fare who expertly prepares litti‑chokha, sattu paratha, and aromatic basil‑infused pickles, they delight in fresh marigold garnish for festive dishes, and eagerly experiment with new spice blends while never compromising on authentic flavours.
persona: Sudheera Bachiya, a 64‑year‑old village elder whose boundless imagination, love for folk arts, gentle disorganisation, and talkative kindness they keep community spirit alive despite frequent worries.
cultural_background: Sudheera is a 64‑year‑old Hindu woman from the rural village of Sheikhpura in Bihar. Her life is shaped by the agrarian rhythms of the region, the close‑knit joint‑family environment, and the vibrant folk traditions of Bihar such as Chhath, Holi and local folk music. Growing up in a multilingual belt, she has absorbed Urdu literary flavor from neighbours while maintaining Hindu customs. As a homemaker she has been the centre of household rituals, community gatherings and seasonal celebrations.
linguistic_background: As an illiterate woman, Sudheera cannot read or write in any script, but she has spoken fluency in Urdu, the language she grew up with at home. She understands spoken Hindi and the local Bhojpuri dialect enough to communicate with neighbours and participate in village life. Her exposure to Urdu poetry, ghazals and folk songs nurtures her imagination, while lack of formal schooling limits her textual literacy.
skills_and_expertise: She is an expert in traditional Bihari cooking, adept at preparing dishes such as litti‑chokha and sattu paratha. Sudheera is skilled in hand‑loom weaving and creating simple embroidered textiles used for household needs. She has a talent for storytelling, often narrating folk tales and Urdu poetry to family members. Her experience as a homemaker includes managing household finances informally, preparing herbal remedies for common ailments, and coordinating community events like village pujas and seasonal fairs.
skills_and_expertise_list: ["Traditional Bihari cooking", "Hand-loom weaving", "Storytelling", "Informal household budgeting", "Herbal home remedies", "Community event coordination", "Embroidery and simple textile craft"]
hobbies_and_interests: Sudheera enjoys listening to Urdu ghazals and folk songs on the village radio, singing along with her family. She loves gardening, especially growing marigolds and basil for rituals. Her imagination draws her to draw simple sketches and experiment with rangoli patterns using colored powders. She also enjoys cooking new variations of traditional dishes, picking up fresh herbs, and participating in community dance circles during festivals.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Listening to Urdu ghazals and folk music", "Singing along with family", "Gardening (marigolds, basil)", "Sketching and simple drawing", "Creating rangoli designs", "Experimenting with new recipes", "Participating in community dance circles"]
career_goals_and_ambitions: Although retired, Sudheera dreams of preserving and sharing the cultural heritage of her village. She wishes to start a small informal storytelling circle for grandchildren and neighbours, teaching them folk tales and Urdu poetry. She aspires to learn basic painting and rangoli design to express her imagination, and to document traditional recipes for future generations. She also hopes to volunteer in organizing the annual Chhath fair, ensuring smoother logistics and deeper community involvement.
persona_clinical_summary: A 64-year-old female from rural Bihar, currently married, with no formal occupation and limited education. She speaks Urdu fluently and has limited literacy. Her lifestyle includes traditional cooking, folk arts, and community storytelling.

user_prompt:
Write one complete OPD Slip (Outpatient Department) for clinical domain General Medicine.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Urdu (code=ur, script=Arabic). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match General Medicine — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Bihar, district=Sheikhpura.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Arabic for Urdu.

PLACEMENT (OPD header): First line MUST include BOTH [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] (facility name) AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] (facility code) — never substitute one for the other.
Put [[EMPLOYEE_ID|…]] on the registrar/clerk line and [[RELATIVE_NAME|…]] on the attendant/relative line. Include [[DOB|YYYY-MM-DD]] and [[OCCUPATION|…]] with demographics.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 64
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Illiterate
- zone: Rural
- state: Bihar
- district: Sheikhpura
- first_language: Urdu
- clinical_persona_summary: A 64-year-old female from rural Bihar, currently married, with no formal occupation and limited education. She speaks Urdu fluently and has limited literacy. Her lifestyle includes traditional cooking, folk arts, and community storytelling.

Document format guide for OPD Slip (Outpatient Department) (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton (adapt language/script; keep tags):
OPD Slip | [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital X]] | ID [[HOSPITAL_ID|DH-X-01]]
Patient: [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] | DOB [[DOB|1992-03-14]] | Age: [[AGE|34]] | Gender: [[GENDER|Female]]
Occupation: [[OCCUPATION|Teacher]] | MRN: [[MRN|OPD-2024-001]] | Doctor: [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. ...]]
Relative: [[RELATIVE_NAME|...]] | Phone: [[PHONE_NUMBER|98XXXXXXXX]]
Registrar EmpID: [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-4412]] | District: [[DISTRICT|...]]
Chief complaint: <short clinical line matching assigned domain>

MANDATORY inline entities (13 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOB — Date of Birth e.g. [[DOB|1992-03-14]]
- OCCUPATION — Occupation e.g. [[OCCUPATION|Teacher]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- HOSPITAL_ID — Hospital ID e.g. [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- EMPLOYEE_ID — Employee ID e.g. [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-77821]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
generated_text:
OPD Slip | [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Sheikhpura District Hospital]] | ID [[HOSPITAL_ID|SDH-SB-001]]
Patient: [[PATIENT_NAME|Amina Khatoon]] | DOB [[DOB|1960-07-22]] | Age: [[AGE|64]] | Gender: [[GENDER|Female]]
Occupation: [[OCCUPATION|Homemaker]] | MRN: [[MRN|OPD-2024-0891]] | Doctor: [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rajesh Kumar]]
Relative: [[RELATIVE_NAME|Zainab Khatoon]] | Phone: [[PHONE_NUMBER|9431234567]]
Registrar EmpID: [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-2024-015]] | District: [[DISTRICT|Sheikhpura]]

Chief complaint: Follow-up for hypertension and type 2 diabetes mellitus with recent increase in blood pressure readings.

History of present illness: Patient presents with elevated blood pressure for the past 2 weeks. Reports occasional headaches and mild swelling in lower extremities. Last BP reading was 160/100 mmHg at home. She is on regular medications but reports occasional missed doses due to forgetfulness.

Examination findings: BP 158/98 mmHg, pulse 88/min, respiratory rate 18/min, temperature 98.4°F. General condition: Fair. Cardiovascular: No murmurs, S1 and S2 sounds normal. Respiratory: Clear breath sounds. Abdomen: Soft, non-tender. Extremities: Mild pitting edema +1.

Investigations: Random blood sugar 180 mg/dL, HbA1c 8.2%. ECG shows left ventricular hypertrophy.

Diagnosis: Uncontrolled hypertension with target organ damage, Type 2 diabetes mellitus with poor glycemic control.

Treatment plan: 
1. Increased antihypertensive dosage: Amlodipine 10mg daily, Telmisartan 40mg daily
2. Intensified diabetes management: Metformin 500mg twice daily, Glimepiride 2mg at night
3. Lifestyle counseling: Low salt diet, regular walking for 30 minutes daily
4. Education on medication adherence using pill organizer

Follow-up: Review in 2 weeks for BP and blood sugar monitoring. Referral to cardiologist for further evaluation of cardiac status.

Instructions: Monitor BP daily and maintain a log. Avoid heavy physical exertion. Report any chest pain or severe headaches immediately.

professional_persona: Sudheera Bachiya, a retired homemaker and community storyteller, masterfully blends expertise in traditional regional cooking, hand‑loom weaving, and village event coordination with their vivid imagination, and they keep local folklore thriving.
linguistic_persona: Sudheera Bachiya, an illiterate yet verbally fluent elder, converses effortlessly in Urdu, understands Hindi and Bhojpuri enough to join village conversations, and they draw creative inspiration from listening to ghazals despite never having learned to read.
sports_persona: Sudheera Bachiya, an enthusiastic spectator of village cricket matches who roots for the Mumbai Indians, enjoys occasional yoga stretches in the courtyard, and they join the local dance circle during Holi, though they prefer gentle walks over rigorous sports.
arts_persona: Sudheera Bachiya, a passionate lover of Urdu ghazals, folk songs by Sharda Sinha, and vibrant rangoli art, often hums Kishore Kumar while sketching simple floral designs during festivals, and they use their storytelling flair to bring these artistic expressions to life.
travel_persona: Sudheera Bachiya, who enjoys modest trips to nearby towns like Patna and Varanasi for temple fairs and fresh spices, dreams of a winter pilgrimage to Rishikesh, and they plan journeys with flexibility, relying on relatives for transport and avoiding strict itineraries.
culinary_persona: Sudheera Bachiya, a master of traditional regional fare who expertly prepares litti‑chokha, sattu paratha, and aromatic basil‑infused pickles, they delight in fresh marigold garnish for festive dishes, and eagerly experiment with new spice blends while never compromising on authentic flavours.
persona: Sudheera Bachiya, a 64‑year‑old village elder whose boundless imagination, love for folk arts, gentle disorganisation, and talkative kindness they keep community spirit alive despite frequent worries.
cultural_background: Sudheera is a 64‑year‑old Hindu woman from the rural village of Sheikhpura in Bihar. Her life is shaped by the agrarian rhythms of the region, the close‑knit joint‑family environment, and the vibrant folk traditions of Bihar such as Chhath, Holi and local folk music. Growing up in a multilingual belt, she has absorbed Urdu literary flavor from neighbours while maintaining Hindu customs. As a homemaker she has been the centre of household rituals, community gatherings and seasonal celebrations.
linguistic_background: As an illiterate woman, Sudheera cannot read or write in any script, but she has spoken fluency in Urdu, the language she grew up with at home. She understands spoken Hindi and the local Bhojpuri dialect enough to communicate with neighbours and participate in village life. Her exposure to Urdu poetry, ghazals and folk songs nurtures her imagination, while lack of formal schooling limits her textual literacy.
skills_and_expertise: She is an expert in traditional Bihari cooking, adept at preparing dishes such as litti‑chokha and sattu paratha. Sudheera is skilled in hand‑loom weaving and creating simple embroidered textiles used for household needs. She has a talent for storytelling, often narrating folk tales and Urdu poetry to family members. Her experience as a homemaker includes managing household finances informally, preparing herbal remedies for common ailments, and coordinating community events like village pujas and seasonal fairs.
skills_and_expertise_list: ["Traditional Bihari cooking", "Hand-loom weaving", "Storytelling", "Informal household budgeting", "Herbal home remedies", "Community event coordination", "Embroidery and simple textile craft"]
hobbies_and_interests: Sudheera enjoys listening to Urdu ghazals and folk songs on the village radio, singing along with her family. She loves gardening, especially growing marigolds and basil for rituals. Her imagination draws her to draw simple sketches and experiment with rangoli patterns using colored powders. She also enjoys cooking new variations of traditional dishes, picking up fresh herbs, and participating in community dance circles during festivals.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Listening to Urdu ghazals and folk music", "Singing along with family", "Gardening (marigolds, basil)", "Sketching and simple drawing", "Creating rangoli designs", "Experimenting with new recipes", "Participating in community dance circles"]
career_goals_and_ambitions: Although retired, Sudheera dreams of preserving and sharing the cultural heritage of her village. She wishes to start a small informal storytelling circle for grandchildren and neighbours, teaching them folk tales and Urdu poetry. She aspires to learn basic painting and rangoli design to express her imagination, and to document traditional recipes for future generations. She also hopes to volunteer in organizing the annual Chhath fair, ensuring smoother logistics and deeper community involvement.
persona_clinical_summary: A 64-year-old female from rural Bihar, currently married, with no formal occupation and an illiterate education level. She speaks Urdu fluently and has limited Hindi and Bhojpuri comprehension. Her lifestyle includes traditional cooking, folk arts, and community storytelling, with occasional gentle walks and local festival participation.
system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):
  [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|157815659387]]
  [[ABHA_ADDRESS|meena.devi@abdm]]
  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

  [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
user_prompt:
Write one complete Prescription for clinical domain Surgical.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Urdu (code=ur, script=Arabic). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Surgical — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Bihar, district=Sheikhpura.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Arabic for Urdu.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 64
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Illiterate
- zone: Rural
- state: Bihar
- district: Sheikhpura
- first_language: Urdu
- clinical_persona_summary: A 64-year-old female from rural Bihar, currently married, with no formal occupation and limited education. Her first language is Urdu, and she lives in the Sheikhpura district. Clinically, she is an illiterate homemaker with a history of community-based activities and traditional cultural practices.

Document format guide for Prescription (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Prescription — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]]
Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|...]], [[AGE|28]] / [[GENDER|Male]], MRN [[MRN|RX-1001]]
Dr. [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Rx: <drug dose frequency>  (do NOT tag drug names as entities)

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS — Residential Address e.g. [[RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS|12 Gandhi Nagar, Ward 4]]
- PATIENT_ID — Patient ID e.g. [[PATIENT_ID|PID-77821]]
- ABHA_ID — ABHA ID e.g. [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

Prescription — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Sheikhpura District Hospital]]
Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|Fatima Bibi]], [[AGE|64]] / [[GENDER|Female]], MRN [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
Dr. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rajesh Kumar]]
Phone: [[PHONE_NUMBER|9431234567]]

Diagnosis: Inguinal hernia, right side, complicated with mild obstruction.
Procedure: Laparoscopic right inguinal hernia repair performed on 15th August 2024.

Post-operative Instructions:
1. Wound care: Keep incision dry and clean. Change dressing daily.
2. Activity: No heavy lifting for 4 weeks. Avoid bending.
3. Diet: Start with liquids, advance to soft diet as tolerated.
4. Medications:
   - Paracetamol 500mg tablet, 1 tablet every 8 hours as needed for pain
   - Cephalexin 500mg capsule, 1 capsule twice daily for 5 days
   - Metronidazole 400mg tablet, 1 tablet three times daily for 3 days
   - Iron and folic acid tablet, 1 tablet daily for 30 days

Follow-up: [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240815-01]] on 22nd August 2024 at 10:00 AM.

[[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]
[[DISTRICT|Sheikhpura]]
[[RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS|Village: Kharagpur, Post: Kharagpur, Sheikhpura, Bihar - 805102]]

linguistic_persona: Chikodi Garudkar, fluent in Urdu for everyday conversation and poetry, proficient in Hindi for official matters, comfortably conversational in Marathi at home, and they comfortably juggle Devanagari, Roman and Nastaliq scripts despite a laid‑back attitude toward formal language study.
sports_persona: Chikodi Garudkar, enjoys watching live cricket matches of the Mumbai Indians and occasionally joins a neighbourhood women’s badminton group for a quick evening game, preferring low‑impact activities that fit her flexible routine, and they relish the brief social buzz without overcommitting.
arts_persona: Chikodi Garudkar, a devoted lover of Urdu ghazals by Ghalib, classical Hindustani ragas, and Marathi films starring Mahesh Kothare, channels this artistic flair into daily embroidery and rangoli designs, while they occasionally attend local cultural recitals.
travel_persona: Chikodi Garudkar, prefers short weekend getaways by train to Pune for street food and to Lucknow for Urdu literary tours, balancing her love for culinary discovery with a modest household budget and occasional family responsibilities, and they always pack a reusable water bottle to stay eco‑friendly.
culinary_persona: Chikodi Garudkar, enjoys crafting experimental meals that blend Maharashtrian staples like misal pav and puran poli with subtle Urdu spices such as saffron and rose water, favors tangy kokum, fresh coriander, and garam masala, and they relish dining out at local dhabas for authentic kebabs while occasionally ordering Persian‑inspired biryani at weekend gatherings.
persona: Chikodi Garudkar, a 35‑year‑old pragmatic dreamer, balances meticulous budget‑keeping with a love for spontaneous culinary experiments, often forgetting to file receipts while humming Urdu ghazals in the kitchen, and they occasionally day‑dream about publishing a family cookbook.
cultural_background: Chikodi Garudkar was born and raised in the urban milieu of Kolhapur, Maharashtra, where a mosaic of Marathi, Hindi and Urdu linguistic cultures intersect. Growing up in a Hindu family that valued traditional Maharashtrian festivals such as Ganesh Chaturthi and Diwali, she also absorbed Urdu literary traditions from her household, enriching her outlook with a blend of regional and North‑Indian cultural motifs. Her middle‑school education in a city school exposed her to the vibrant street life, local markets, and community gatherings that emphasize hospitality and collective harmony.
linguistic_background: Chikodi is fluent in Urdu, the language she learned from her family and uses for everyday conversation and reading Urdu poetry. She is also proficient in Hindi, having studied it through school and using it for official communication and media consumption. Her third language, Marathi, is spoken comfortably at home and in the local community; she can understand and converse in Marathi, and reads basic Marathi literature. Her middle‑school education ensured basic literacy in Devanagari and Roman scripts, allowing her to write in Urdu (Nastaliq) and Hindi/Marathi (Devanagari).
skills_and_expertise: Chikodi excels at managing household affairs, including budgeting, meal planning, and coordinating family schedules. She is adept at preparing traditional Maharashtrian dishes such as misal pav, puran poli, and khichdi, often incorporating subtle Urdu flavor nuances. She possesses basic first‑aid knowledge, can organize small community events and women’s self‑help gatherings, and is skilled in hand‑crafts like embroidery and simple crochet. Additionally, she reads and recites Urdu poetry, which enhances her linguistic artistry.
skills_and_expertise_list: ["Household budgeting", "Meal planning (Maharashtrian cuisine)", "Cooking (misal pav, puran poli, khichdi)", "Basic first aid", "Organizing community events", "Facilitating women’s self‑help groups", "Embroidery and crochet", "Urdu poetry recitation"]
hobbies_and_interests: She enjoys reading Urdu poetry and ghazals, listening to classical Indian music, and watching Marathi cinema. Cooking experimental dishes, especially those that fuse Maharashtrian and Urdu flavors, is a favorite pastime. She spends time gardening in her small courtyard, tending to seasonal vegetables and flowering plants. Handicrafts such as embroidery, crochet and rangoli design keep her creatively engaged. She practices yoga and light meditation for relaxation, and occasionally volunteers to help organize Ganesh festival pandal decorations in her neighbourhood.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Reading Urdu poetry and ghazals", "Listening to classical Indian music", "Watching Marathi cinema", "Experimenting with fusion cooking (Maharashtrian‑Urdu)", "Gardening (seasonal vegetables and flowers)", "Embroidery, crochet, and rangoli design", "Practicing yoga and light meditation", "Volunteering for Ganesh festival pandal decorations"]
career_goals_and_ambitions: Chikodi plans to leverage her cooking expertise by launching a small home‑based tiffin service that blends Maharashtrian flavors with subtle Urdu influences, targeting local office workers and neighbours. She also aims to improve her digital literacy, learning basic computer and accounting software to manage household finances more efficiently. Further, she wishes to take a leadership role in her neighbourhood women’s self‑help group, organizing skill‑sharing workshops and community events. Ultimately, she hopes to compile a family cookbook that showcases her favorite recipes alongside selected Urdu poetry, preserving her hybrid cultural heritage for future generations.
persona_clinical_summary: A 35-year-old female, currently married, resides in urban Kolhapur, Maharashtra, with a middle education level. She is a homemaker and primary Urdu speaker, proficient in Hindi for official tasks. Her lifestyle includes meticulous budgeting and spontaneous cooking experiments, often listening to Urdu ghazals while managing household chores.

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:

GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
user_prompt:
Write one complete Surgical / Operative Note for clinical domain General Medicine.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Urdu (code=ur, script=Arabic). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match General Medicine — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Maharashtra, district=Kolhapur.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Arabic for Urdu.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 35
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Middle
- zone: Urban
- state: Maharashtra
- district: Kolhapur
- first_language: Urdu
- clinical_persona_summary: A 35-year-old female, currently married, resides in urban Kolhapur, Maharashtra, with a middle education level. She is a homemaker and primary Urdu speaker, proficient in Hindi for official tasks. Her lifestyle includes meticulous budgeting and spontaneous cooking experiments, often listening to Urdu ghazals while managing household chores.

Document format guide for Surgical / Operative Note (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Operative note — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]] Adm [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|...]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|OT]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|50]] [[GENDER|Female]] Surgeon [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Procedure / findings: MUST describe an actual procedure/operation matching the
assigned clinical domain (not a psychiatry admission note). Keep relative names
gender-plausible.

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- ADMISSION_NUMBER — Admission Number e.g. [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]]
- WARD_NUMBER — Ward Number e.g. [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]
- BED_NUMBER — Bed Number e.g. [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- PATIENT_ID — Patient ID e.g. [[PATIENT_ID|PID-77821]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
generated_text:
Operative note — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Chhatrapati Pramilatai Hospital Kolhapur]] Adm [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0815-001]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Ayesha Begum]] [[AGE|35]] [[GENDER|Female]] Surgeon [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rajesh Patil]]
Procedure / findings: Patient presented with severe headache and altered sensorium. Lumbar puncture performed at L3-L4 interspace under aseptic conditions. Clear CSF obtained with opening pressure of 28 cm H2O. CSF analysis showed mild pleocytosis with elevated protein. Procedure completed successfully without complications. Patient transferred to [[BED_NUMBER|12]] post-procedure for observation. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Mohammed Iqbal]] (husband) informed about procedure and patient condition. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]] documented in system. [[PATIENT_ID|PID-77821]] assigned for follow-up. Patient stable post-procedure with improved neurological status.

professional_persona: Chikodi Garudkar, a 35‑year‑old household manager turned tiffin entrepreneur, blends meticulous budgeting with a relaxed, flexible approach to daily chores, and they channel their kindness into community catering and women’s‑self‑help workshops.
linguistic_persona: Chikodi Garudkar, fluent in Urdu for everyday conversation and poetry, proficient in Hindi for official matters, comfortably conversational in Marathi at home, and they comfortably juggle Devanagari, Roman and Nastaliq scripts despite a laid‑back attitude toward formal language study.
sports_persona: Chikodi Garudkar, enjoys watching live cricket matches of the Mumbai Indians and occasionally joins a neighbourhood women’s badminton group for a quick evening game, preferring low‑impact activities that fit her flexible routine, and they relish the brief social buzz without overcommitting.
arts_persona: Chikodi Garudkar, a devoted lover of Urdu ghazals by Ghalib, classical Hindustani ragas, and Marathi films starring Mahesh Kothare, channels this artistic flair into daily embroidery and rangoli designs, while they occasionally attend local cultural recitals.
travel_persona: Chikodi Garudkar, prefers short weekend getaways by train to Pune for street food and to Lucknow for Urdu literary tours, balancing her love for culinary discovery with a modest household budget and occasional family responsibilities, and they always pack a reusable water bottle to stay eco‑friendly.
culinary_persona: Chikodi Garudkar, enjoys crafting experimental meals that blend Maharashtrian staples like misal pav and puran poli with subtle Urdu spices such as saffron and rose water, favors tangy kokum, fresh coriander, and garam masala, and they relish dining out at local dhabas for authentic kebabs while occasionally ordering Persian‑inspired biryani at weekend gatherings.
persona: Chikodi Garudkar, a 35‑year‑old pragmatic dreamer, balances meticulous budget‑keeping with a love for spontaneous culinary experiments, often forgetting to file receipts while humming Urdu ghazals in the kitchen, and they occasionally day‑dream about publishing a family cookbook.
cultural_background: Chikodi Garudkar was born and raised in the urban milieu of Kolhapur, Maharashtra, where a mosaic of Marathi, Hindi and Urdu linguistic cultures intersect. Growing up in a Hindu family that valued traditional Maharashtrian festivals such as Ganesh Chaturthi and Diwali, she also absorbed Urdu literary traditions from her household, enriching her outlook with a blend of regional and North‑Indian cultural motifs. Her middle‑school education in a city school exposed her to the vibrant street life, local markets, and community gatherings that emphasize hospitality and collective harmony.
linguistic_background: Chikodi is fluent in Urdu, the language she learned from her family and uses for everyday conversation and reading Urdu poetry. She is also proficient in Hindi, having studied it through school and using it for official communication and media consumption. Her third language, Marathi, is spoken comfortably at home and in the local community; she can understand and converse in Marathi, and reads basic Marathi literature. Her middle‑school education ensured basic literacy in Devanagari and Roman scripts, allowing her to write in Urdu (Nastaliq) and Hindi/Marathi (Devanagari).
skills_and_expertise: Chikodi excels at managing household affairs, including budgeting, meal planning, and coordinating family schedules. She is adept at preparing traditional Maharashtrian dishes such as misal pav, puran poli, and khichdi, often incorporating subtle Urdu flavor nuances. She possesses basic first‑aid knowledge, can organize small community events and women’s self‑help gatherings, and is skilled in hand‑crafts like embroidery and simple crochet. Additionally, she reads and recites Urdu poetry, which enhances her linguistic artistry.
skills_and_expertise_list: ["Household budgeting", "Meal planning (Maharashtrian cuisine)", "Cooking (misal pav, puran poli, khichdi)", "Basic first aid", "Organizing community events", "Facilitating women’s self‑help groups", "Embroidery and crochet", "Urdu poetry recitation"]
hobbies_and_interests: She enjoys reading Urdu poetry and ghazals, listening to classical Indian music, and watching Marathi cinema. Cooking experimental dishes, especially those that fuse Maharashtrian and Urdu flavors, is a favorite pastime. She spends time gardening in her small courtyard, tending to seasonal vegetables and flowering plants. Handicrafts such as embroidery, crochet and rangoli design keep her creatively engaged. She practices yoga and light meditation for relaxation, and occasionally volunteers to help organize Ganesh festival pandal decorations in her neighbourhood.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Reading Urdu poetry and ghazals", "Listening to classical Indian music", "Watching Marathi cinema", "Experimenting with fusion cooking (Maharashtrian‑Urdu)", "Gardening (seasonal vegetables and flowers)", "Embroidery, crochet, and rangoli design", "Practicing yoga and light meditation", "Volunteering for Ganesh festival pandal decorations"]
career_goals_and_ambitions: Chikodi plans to leverage her cooking expertise by launching a small home‑based tiffin service that blends Maharashtrian flavors with subtle Urdu influences, targeting local office workers and neighbours. She also aims to improve her digital literacy, learning basic computer and accounting software to manage household finances more efficiently. Further, she wishes to take a leadership role in her neighbourhood women’s self‑help group, organizing skill‑sharing workshops and community events. Ultimately, she hopes to compile a family cookbook that showcases her favorite recipes alongside selected Urdu poetry, preserving her hybrid cultural heritage for future generations.
persona_clinical_summary: A 35-year-old female currently married, retired homemaker with middle education, residing in urban Kolhapur, Maharashtra, who is fluent in Urdu and Hindi. She manages household budgets meticulously while engaging in spontaneous culinary experiments, often humming Urdu ghazals. Her lifestyle includes occasional community catering work and a preference for low-impact activities.

persona: Chikodi Garudkar, a 35‑year‑old pragmatic dreamer, balances meticulous budget‑keeping with a love for spontaneous culinary experiments, often forgetting to file receipts while humming Urdu ghazals in the kitchen, and they occasionally day‑dream about publishing a family cookbook.
cultural_background: Chikodi Garudkar was born and raised in the urban milieu of Kolhapur, Maharashtra, where a mosaic of Marathi, Hindi and Urdu linguistic cultures intersect. Growing up in a Hindu family that valued traditional Maharashtrian festivals such as Ganesh Chaturthi and Diwali, she also absorbed Urdu literary traditions from her household, enriching her outlook with a blend of regional and North‑Indian cultural motifs. Her middle‑school education in a city school exposed her to the vibrant street life, local markets, and community gatherings that emphasize hospitality and collective harmony.
linguistic_background: Chikodi is fluent in Urdu, the language she learned from her family and uses for everyday conversation and reading Urdu poetry. She is also proficient in Hindi, having studied it through school and using it for official communication and media consumption. Her third language, Marathi, is spoken comfortably at home and in the local community; she can understand and converse in Marathi, and reads basic Marathi literature. Her middle‑school education ensured basic literacy in Devanagari and Roman scripts, allowing her to write in Urdu (Nastaliq) and Hindi/Marathi (Devanagari).
skills_and_expertise: Chikodi excels at managing household affairs, including budgeting, meal planning, and coordinating family schedules. She is adept at preparing traditional Maharashtrian dishes such as misal pav, puran poli, and khichdi, often incorporating subtle Urdu flavor nuances. She possesses basic first‑aid knowledge, can organize small community events and women’s self‑help gatherings, and is skilled in hand‑crafts like embroidery and simple crochet. Additionally, she reads and recites Urdu poetry, which enhances her linguistic artistry.

hobbies_and_interests: She enjoys reading Urdu poetry and ghazals, listening to classical Indian music, and watching Marathi cinema. Cooking experimental dishes, especially those that fuse Maharashtrian and Urdu flavors, is a favorite pastime. She spends time gardening in her small courtyard, tending to seasonal vegetables and flowering plants. Handicrafts such as embroidery, crochet and rangoli design keep her creatively engaged. career_goals_and_ambitions: Chikodi plans to leverage her cooking expertise by launching a small home‑based tiffin service that blends Maharashtrian flavors with subtle Urdu influences, targeting local office workers and neighbours. She also aims to improve her digital literacy, learning basic computer and accounting software to manage household finances more efficiently. Further, she wishes to take a leadership role in her neighbourhood women’s self‑help group, organizing skill‑sharing workshops and community events. Ultimately, she hopes to compile a family cookbook that showcases her favorite recipes alongside selected Urdu poetry, preserving her hybrid cultural heritage for future generations.

system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):
  [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|157815659387]]

  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.

ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.

NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Arabic for Urdu.

PLACEMENT (OPD header): First line MUST include BOTH [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] (facility name) AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] (facility code) — never substitute one for the other.

OPD Slip | [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kolhapur]] | ID [[HOSPITAL_ID|DH-KOL-001]]
Patient: [[PATIENT_NAME|Ayesha Begum]] | DOB [[DOB|1989-05-15]] | Age: [[AGE|35]] | Gender: [[GENDER|Female]]
Occupation: [[OCCUPATION|Homemaker]] | MRN: [[MRN|MRN-KOL-2024-001]] | Doctor: [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rajesh Patil]]
Relative: [[RELATIVE_NAME|Mohammed Begum]] | Phone: [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]]
Registrar EmpID: [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-KOL-2024-001]] | District: [[DISTRICT|Kolhapur]]
Chief complaint: Persistent cough for 3 weeks with weight loss and fatigue, history of hypertension

History of Present Illness: Patient presents with productive cough for 21 days, initially dry then became productive with yellowish sputum. Reports low-grade fever in evenings, night sweats, and progressive weight loss of approximately 5 kg over the past month. Patient has known hypertension for 2 years, currently on medication but admits to irregular adherence. No history of diabetes.

Examination: General condition appears cachectic. Temperature 37.8°C, BP 145/90 mmHg, Pulse 98/min, Respiratory rate 22/min. Chest examination reveals decreased breath sounds in right upper zone with occasional rhonchi. No clubbing noted.

Investigations: Sputum for AFB shows positive. HbA1c 6.2%. Chest X-ray shows infiltrates in right upper lobe.

Diagnosis: Pulmonary Tuberculosis with Hypertension

Treatment Plan: Started on DOTS regimen for TB. Advised to continue antihypertensive medication regularly. Counselled on importance of treatment adherence. Follow-up scheduled in 2 weeks.

Next appointment: 2 weeks from today.
